problems.statement: A 10hp pump with 90% effciency transports water to an elevation of 200ft above the pump. What volume of water is transported in a 1 hour period?
problems.solution: of 200ft above the pump. What volume of water is transported in a 1 hour period? A. 300gallons B. 1400gallons C. 10,700gallons D. 11,900gallons Start byconsidering therelationshipbetweenpower, work, and energy. Notethat work andenergy have common units. Work is typically represented as a force exerted over a distance. For a mass of water being raised by a certain distance, it must experience an increase in Potential Energy: mgh PE = g c Using m=ρV mgh ρVgh PE = = g g c c Equatethepotentialenergyaddedtothewatertotheamountofworkdone,whichisafunction of the power and the time. W P = →W =Pt t Since the pump has a stated e(cid:30)ciency, apply a multiplier with the pump horsepower rating: W =ηPt Equate the work and potential energy: ρVgh ηPt= g c Solve for volume, then substitute values. Find the conversion from hp to ft·lbf by searching sec Measurement Relationships. (cid:16) (cid:17) (cid:16) (cid:17)(cid:16) (cid:17) (.9)(10hp) 550ft−lbf (3600sec) 32.2 lbm−ft 7.48gallons V = ηPtg c = sec−hp lbf−sec2 ft3 =10,680gallons (cid:16) (cid:17)(cid:16) (cid:17) ρgh 62.4lbm 32.2 ft (200ft) ft3 sec2 Answer C 164

problems.statement: Water at 400psi and 700◦F is in what thermodynamic state?
problems.solution: A. Liquid B. Saturated Mixture C. Saturated Vapor D. Superheated Vapor UsethePropertiesofSaturatedWaterandSteamtableandPropertiesofSuperheatedSteamtable to check where enthalpy values are given for the stated pressure and temperature. Using the saturated water table by pressure and (cid:28)nding 400psi, the corresponding temperature is lower than 700◦F. Therefore, at the stated temperature and pressure, water will be a superheated vapor. Answer D

problems.solution: 20◦F of superheat. What is the entropy of the refrigerant entering the compressor? A. 0.03Btu lb◦F B. 0.22Btu lb◦F C. 0.24Btu lb◦F D. 106Btu lb◦F In the Reference Handbook, search for Properties of Refrigerants and use the phrase: Pressure Versus Enthalpy. Find the chart for Refrigerant 134a. Followingahorizontallineatapressureof20psiatotherightmostedgeofthesaturationcurve, corresponding to a saturated vapor condition. Continue horizontally to the right to account for the additional 20◦F of superheat. Note that to the right of the saturation curve, lines of constant temperature are curved downward (nearly vertical). This is the compressor entering condition, typically noted as State 1 of the refrigeration cycle. Once the state point has been identi(cid:28)ed, read o(cid:27) the entropy from the chart. Note that lines of constant entropy run roughly southwest/northeast on the diagram. Btu s≈0.235 lb◦F Answer C

problems.statement: Atmospheric air at 77◦F undergoes isentropic compression to 100psia and 470◦F. How much work is done to the system during the process? lb lb lb lb

A. 0.5Btu lb B. 70Btu lb C. 90Btu lb D. 50,000Btu lb ForanIsentropicProcess, thereare(atleast)twoformulasthatmaybeusedto(cid:28)ndtheworkdone from one state to another, which can be shown to be equivalent: P v −P v w = 2 2 1 1 1−k R(T −T ) w = 2 1 1−k Using the ideal gas law and solving for speci(cid:28)c volume: RT PV =mRT →Pv =RT →v = P Substitute for speci(cid:28)c volume in the (cid:28)rst formula and cancel pressure: (cid:16) (cid:17) (cid:16) (cid:17) w = P 2 R P T 2 2 −P 1 R P T 1 1 = R(T 2 −T 1 ) 1−k 1−k For convenience, use the second equation which is a function of temperature only: 170 (cid:16) (cid:17) w = R(T 2 −T 1 ) = 53.35 l f b t m ·l · b R f [470◦F −77◦F] = −52,416f l t b · m lbf =−67 Btu 1−k 1−1.4 778ft·lbf lb m Btu NormallyitwouldberequiredtochangeFarenheittoRankine;however,sinceitisatemperature di(cid:27)erence, the delta is unchanged. Note the unit conversion from ft·lb to Btu. f Finally, note the negative sign of the answer which aligns with the question’s implication that work is being done to the system. A positive value would be expected if work were being done by the system. Answer B

problems.statement: An outside air handling unit delivers 5000cfm of outside air. On a winter day, the outside air is 18◦F and is heated to 68◦F using a hot water coil. Hot water is supplied at 165◦F and returned at 140◦F. What is the required flow rate of hot water?
problems.solution: a winter day, the outside air is 18◦F and is heated to 68◦F using a hot water coil. Hot water is supplied at 165◦F and returned at 140◦F. What is the required (cid:29)ow rate of hot water? A. 0.4gpm B. 3gpm C. 22gpm D. 173gpm The air undergoes sensible heating. Use the sensible heating rule of thumb: Q =1.08CFM∆T s air The heat given up by the water can be determined with the sensible heating/cooling rule of thumb for water: Q =500GPM∆T w water The quantity of heat added to the air is supplied entirely by the hot water, therefore we can equate the two formulas: 1.08CFM∆T =500GPM∆T air water The GPM of the hot water is the only unknown. Isolate, substitute, and solve: 1.08CFM∆T 1.08(5000)(68−18) GPM = air = =21.6GPM 500∆T 500(165−140) water Note that no units need to be included when using these (cid:16)rules of thumb(cid:17) provided all input values are in the correct units in the (cid:28)rst place i.e. CFM, GPM, and Farenheit. Answer C 172

Since the answer choices are total work [Btu], multiply by the total mass in the room. Use the ideal gas law and either state. State 1 is arbitrarily chosen here. problems.statement: A Carnot heat pump operates between 20◦F and 70◦F. What is the coeffcient of performance? 19
problems.solution: the coe(cid:30)cient of performance? A. 0.4 B. 1.4 C. 9.6 D. 10.6 Look up the Carnot Cycle in the reference handbook and (cid:28)nd the Coe(cid:30)cient of Performance formulas. A Carnot heat pump operates at the upper limit of COP, which is a function of the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs which the heat pump is operating between. T COP = H HP,carnot (T −T ) H L The temperatures must be in absolute terms i.e. Rankine, so add 460 to ◦F before substituting into the formula. (Technically, there is no need to change to absolute in the denominator since the di(cid:27)erence will remain unchanged.) T (70+460) 530R COP = H = = =10.6 HP,carnot (T −T ) (70−20) 50R H L Answer D

problems.statement: In a Carnot heat pump, R-22 evaporates at 31psia and condenses at 211psia. What is the coeffcient of performance?
problems.solution: at 211psia. What is the coe(cid:30)cient of performance? A. 0.9 B. 4.3 C. 5.1 D. 6.8 Lookup the Pressure Versus Enthalpy Curves for Refrigerant 22 and read from the chart the tem- peratures for the evaporator and condenser based on the pressures given. These correspond to the low and high temperature reservoirs, respectively, for the heat pump: T =T ≈−10◦F +460=450◦R L evaporator@P=31psia T =T ≈100◦F +460=560◦R H condenser@P=211psia Look up the Carnot Cycle in the reference handbook and (cid:28)nd the coe(cid:30)cient of performance formula: T 560◦R COP = H = =5.1 HP,carnot (T −T ) 560◦R−450◦R H L Answer C 175

To start, bring up the steam table in the reference handbook by searching for Properties of Superheated Steam and Properties of Saturated Water (Pressure) and determine the enthalpy entering and leaving the turbine. Call the entering condition State 1 and the leaving condition State 2. State 1, The superheated steam is fully de(cid:28)ned since the temperature and pressure are known. In addition to the enthalpy, note the entropy for State 1. T =900◦F 1 P =500psia 1 Btu h =1466.9 1 lb Btu s =1.699 1 lb◦F State 2, Start by assuming isentropic (ideal) expansion in the turbine. Use the entropy to determine the quality, and use the quality to determine the enthalpy for State 2 ideal. In the subsequent step we will distinguish State 2 actual. Since the goal is to produce output power, a less e(cid:30)cientturbinewillputoutlessthanitcouldifitwere100%e(cid:30)cient. A. $2670 B. $3490 C. $3750 D. $5030 The cost is a function of electrical power and time, and electrical power is a function of hydraulic horsepower (aka water horsepower i.e. whp) and e(cid:30)ciency. Start by calculating the water horse- power based on volume (cid:29)ow rate and feet of head provided by the pump: Q∆h (200)(130) whp= = =6.566hp 3960 3960 Note the volume and head units must be in GPM and feet, respectively, to use this (cid:16)rule of thumb(cid:17) equation. Therefore units need not be shown, provided they are con(cid:28)rmed to be correct prior to use. Recall that brake horsepower, bhp, depends on water horsepower, whp, and the e(cid:30)ciency of the pump, η . Similarly, the electrical power, P ,depends on brake horsepower, bhp, and motor p elec e(cid:30)ciency, η . m whp bhp= η p bhp P = elec η m Put these together, substitute, solve, and convert to KW: (cid:18) (cid:19) whp 6.566hp .746KW P = = =6.58KW elec η η (.8)(.93) 1hp p m To (cid:28)nd the annual cost, multiply by time and the unit rate of electricity: (cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19) 12hrs 5days 52wks $0.13 Cost=(6.58KW) =$2669peryear day wk yr KWH Answer A 187

problems.statement: 10,000gal of fuel oil with a specific gravity of 0.86 is delivered to an hr emergency generator by a 90% effcient pump. The pressure gauge near the pump inlet reads −2.0inHg. The pressure gauge on the discharge side of the pump reads 30psig and is located 10ft above the pump. What is the input power to the pump?
problems.solution:
This problem combines several fluids concepts: unit conversion, Bernoulli equation for a non-water fluid, and pump power.

Flow rate: Q = 10,000 gal/hr × (1 hr/60 min) = 166.67 GPM.

Inlet pressure (P₁): Given as −2.0 inches of mercury (gauge, vacuum). Convert:
−2.0 inHg × (13.6 inH₂O / 1 inHg) = −27.2 inH₂O
−27.2 inH₂O × (1 ft / 12 in) = −2.267 ftH₂O
−2.267 ftH₂O × (1 PSI / 2.31 ftH₂O) = −0.98 PSI (gauge)

Discharge pressure (P₂): 30 psig at 10 ft above pump. The 10 ft column is fuel oil, not water:
10 ft fuel × SG(0.86) = 8.6 ft equivalent water
8.6 ftH₂O / 2.31 = 3.72 PSI
P₂ = 30 + 3.72 = 33.72 PSI (gauge)

ΔP = P₂ − P₁ = 33.72 − (−0.98) = 34.7 PSI

Hydraulic horsepower: WHP = Q × ΔP / 1714 = 166.67 × 34.7 / 1714 = 3.37 hp.
(This formula works for any fluid when ΔP is in PSI — no SG needed.)

Break horsepower: BHP = WHP / η_pump = 3.37 / 0.90 = 3.74 hp → Answer D.

Common mistake: dividing by 3960 instead of 1714 (3960 is for head in feet of water), or multiplying by efficiency instead of dividing (which gives 3.0 hp → wrong answer).

problems.solution_full:
This problem combines several fluids concepts: unit conversion, Bernoulli equation for a non-water fluid, and pump power.

Flow rate: Q = 10,000 gal/hr × (1 hr/60 min) = 166.67 GPM.

Inlet pressure (P₁): Given as −2.0 inches of mercury (gauge, vacuum). Convert:
−2.0 inHg × (13.6 inH₂O / 1 inHg) = −27.2 inH₂O
−27.2 inH₂O × (1 ft / 12 in) = −2.267 ftH₂O
−2.267 ftH₂O × (1 PSI / 2.31 ftH₂O) = −0.98 PSI (gauge)

Discharge pressure (P₂): 30 psig at 10 ft above pump. The 10 ft column is fuel oil, not water:
10 ft fuel × SG(0.86) = 8.6 ft equivalent water
8.6 ftH₂O / 2.31 = 3.72 PSI
P₂ = 30 + 3.72 = 33.72 PSI (gauge)

ΔP = P₂ − P₁ = 33.72 − (−0.98) = 34.7 PSI

Hydraulic horsepower: WHP = Q × ΔP / 1714 = 166.67 × 34.7 / 1714 = 3.37 hp.
(This formula works for any fluid when ΔP is in PSI — no SG needed.)

Break horsepower: BHP = WHP / η_pump = 3.37 / 0.90 = 3.74 hp → Answer D.

Common mistake: dividing by 3960 instead of 1714 (3960 is for head in feet of water), or multiplying by efficiency instead of dividing (which gives 3.0 hp → wrong answer).
problems.solution_summary:
Step 1: Convert flow rate: Q = 10,000 gal/hr ÷ 60 = 166.7 GPM

Step 2: Convert inlet pressure to PSI: −2.0 inHg → −27.2 inH₂O → −0.98 PSI (vacuum)

Step 3: Account for discharge elevation: 10 ft of fuel oil (SG 0.86) = 8.6 ft equivalent water = 3.72 PSI. Total discharge: 30 + 3.72 = 33.72 PSI

Step 4: Calculate pressure differential: ΔP = 33.72 − (−0.98) = 34.7 PSI

Step 5: Find water horsepower: WHP = Q × ΔP / 1714 = 166.7 × 34.7 / 1714 = 3.37 hp

Step 6: Find break horsepower (input power): BHP = WHP / η = 3.37 / 0.90 = 3.74 hp → Answer D (3.8 hp)
problems.video_synthesis:
Dan's video solution lays out the three approaches clearly:

1. **PSI method (recommended):** Convert everything to PSI, use WHP = Q·ΔP/1714. No SG term needed because pressure already accounts for fluid density. This is the cleanest path.

2. **Feet-of-water method:** Convert pressures to feet of water, use WHP = Q·ΔH/3960. Must ensure all heights are in equivalent feet of water.

3. **Feet-of-fluid method:** Keep everything in feet of the actual fluid, then use BHP = Q·ΔH·SG/(3960·η).

The video stresses: pick ONE approach and commit. The classic error is double-counting SG — adjusting the 10 ft column AND including SG in the final formula. Also notes: divide by efficiency (not multiply) because BHP > WHP. Multiplying by 0.9 gives ~3.0 hp (wrong); dividing gives 3.74 hp → answer D.

oh_questions_full.theme: Double-counting specific gravity — the #1 error on this problem
oh_questions_full.question: I used feet of water instead of PSI and I keep getting the wrong answer. I think I'm double-counting the specific gravity somewhere. Can you show where the SG belongs?
oh_questions_full.answer: This is far and away the most common question on Fluids 14 — I've probably answered a version of it on half the office hours since we started. The issue: if you write Bernoulli in feet of head, every term is in feet of the actual fluid (fuel oil, not water). The 10 ft elevation is already in feet of fuel oil — do NOT multiply by SG again. Then at the end, BHP = Q·ΔH·SG/(3960·η) handles the water-equivalence conversion. Alternatively — and this is my recommended approach — convert everything to PSI first and use Q·ΔP/1714. No SG needed at all, because a PSI is a PSI regardless of what fluid created it. You have to commit to one approach and stick to it ruthlessly. The trouble comes from doing SG in one term of Bernoulli (like the elevation) but not the others (like the static pressure conversion), then also including it in the final formula. You can't mix approaches.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: The issue here that's coming up is the potential double counting of the specific gravity... you sort of have to make a decision. Are you going to use the pressure units all the way? Or are you going to use feet all the way?

oh_questions_full.theme: Understanding the 2.31 rule of thumb — and why it breaks for non-water fluids
oh_questions_full.question: I tried to convert inches of mercury to feet using 2.31, but the fluid isn't water. Where does 2.31 come from and what do I use instead?
oh_questions_full.answer: The 2.31 comes from 144 in²/ft² divided by 62.4 lb/ft³ (the specific weight of water). So it has water's density baked in. If the fluid isn't water, you can't use 2.31 directly. For fuel oil with SG = 0.86, the equivalent constant would be 144/53.6 ≈ 2.69. But I don't recommend inventing new constants for every fluid — that's forcing it. Instead, keep things in pressure units (PSI). Convert the inHg reading: −2.0 inHg × 13.6 inH₂O/inHg = −27.2 inH₂O, then to feet of water, then to PSI (÷ 2.31). For the elevation term, use P = γ·z: specific weight of fuel oil (53.6 lb/ft³) × 10 ft ÷ 144 = PSI. Then add in PSI. Once everything is in PSI, use Q·ΔP/1714 and you're done — no SG needed.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: I think the rule of thumb is for water. And if we're dealing with water, we should use it. And if we're not dealing with water, then we shouldn't try to use it.

oh_questions_full.theme: The three power formulas — when to use 1714 vs 3960 vs the general form
oh_questions_full.question: The reference handbook has BHP = GPM·ΔH·SG/(3960·η). Your solution uses Q·ΔP/1714. And someone mentioned W = Q·γ·H/η. Are these all the same? How do I pick?
oh_questions_full.answer:
They're all the same physics expressed differently. Here's the decision tree:

• Q·ΔP/1714: Works for ANY fluid. Q in GPM, ΔP in PSI. No SG term needed. This is the pre-packaged shortcut I recommend — it's ready-made, no unit conversion headaches.

• Q·ΔH/3960: ONLY for water. ΔH in feet of water. The 3960 = 1714 × 2.31, so water's feet-to-PSI conversion is baked in.

• Q·ΔH·SG/(3960·η): Works for any fluid. ΔH in feet of the actual fluid (not water). The SG bridges between 'feet of non-water fluid' and the water-based constant 3960.

• W = Q·γ·H/η: The most general form — works for anything, but you have to do all the unit conversions yourself (GPM to ft³/s, γ in lbf/ft³, g_c, etc.). It's correct but slower.

Notice that 3960 ÷ 1714 = 2.31. That's not a coincidence. Pick one approach, understand what each term means, and don't mix formulas.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: These formulas come up a lot... If you're ever worried about 'do I have to multiply by specific gravity' — if you can get to PSI, then you don't have to. But to get to PSI, you might have to use the specific gravity.

oh_questions_full.theme: Unit conversion chain: inHg → inH₂O → ftH₂O → PSI (and gauge vs absolute)
oh_questions_full.question: I'm struggling with the inlet pressure conversion from −2.0 inHg. Should I also worry about gauge vs absolute pressure here?
oh_questions_full.answer: The conversion chain is: −2.0 inHg × 13.6 inH₂O/inHg = −27.2 inH₂O → ÷ 12 = −2.267 ftH₂O → ÷ 2.31 = −0.98 PSI gauge. The negative sign means it's a vacuum (suction side of the pump). For this problem, you can stay in gauge pressure throughout because both readings are gauge — the atmospheric pressure cancels when you take ΔP. The distinction between gauge and absolute matters when your formula requires absolute pressure, but Q·ΔP/1714 works fine with gauge as long as both sides are gauge. Don't add 14.7 unless you have a reason to. And a broader tip: when you see pressure in atmospheres or want to convert to atmospheres — that's fine, it's a perfectly valid unit. But PSI is generally more practical for this exam because most formulas use it directly.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: Once you're clear on whether you're dealing with gauge or absolute, you are welcome to use any pressure units that you prefer... you should do that with the confidence of knowing that it all works out in the end as long as you show all your units.

oh_questions_full.theme: Efficiency direction: divide by η, don't multiply (BHP > WHP)
oh_questions_full.question: I keep getting confused about whether to multiply or divide by pump efficiency. I got 3.0 hp but the answer is 3.8.
oh_questions_full.answer: Divide by efficiency, never multiply. BHP = WHP / η. The break horsepower (what the motor actually delivers to the pump shaft) is always MORE than the water/hydraulic horsepower (the useful fluid power) because some energy is lost. If η = 0.9, then BHP = 3.37/0.9 = 3.74 hp → answer D. If you multiply by 0.9, you get 3.03 hp, which would be answer B or C — that's saying the pump is magically losing power by having an imperfect efficiency, which doesn't make physical sense. Think of it this way: you have to put in MORE power than comes out. The efficiency tells you the fraction that comes out. So if 90% comes out as useful work, you had to put in more than that: input = output / 0.9.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: The break horsepower is all of the mechanical work... the water horse power is only a fraction of the breakhorse power, and that fraction is the efficiency.

oh_questions_full.theme: Three formula variants — which one to use and when
oh_questions_full.question: Could you also use W = Q·γ·H / η instead of Q·ΔP / 1714?
oh_questions_full.answer: Both are mathematically equivalent, but the PSI method is strongly preferred. It's 'ready made'—requires only GPM and PSI, works for any fluid, and avoids the gamma and specific weight unit conversion headaches.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: This formula is preferred because it's just ready made as long as you have the volume flow rate in GPM and the delta P in PSI. Doesn't matter what the fluid is, you can use this formula.

oh_questions_full.theme: Specific gravity application: 0.86 → 8.6 feet of water equivalent
oh_questions_full.question: Where does the conversion 0.86 feet of water per foot of head come from in the specific gravity?
oh_questions_full.answer: Specific gravity (SG) = (density of fluid) / (density of water). If oil has SG = 0.86, then 100 feet of oil exerts the same pressure as 86 feet of water. So 10 ft of oil (SG 0.86) = 10 × 0.86 = 8.6 feet of water equivalent. Not a conversion factor; it's the definition of specific gravity applied to pressure calculations.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: If we have a column of oil, it's 100 feet of oil. Or we could say it's 86 feet of water. Oil has a specific gravity of 86% that of water. So we could say that it's 100 feet of oil, or we could say that it's 86 feet of water.

oh_questions_full.theme: Reference handbook formula variations and why they differ
oh_questions_full.question: The handbook shows BHP = GPM·ΔH·SG/(3960·η). How does this relate to Q·ΔP/1714 for a non-water fluid?
oh_questions_full.answer: Same physics, three equivalent forms: (1) BHP = Q·H·SG/3960/η (feet of fluid head); (2) BHP = Q·H/3960/η (feet of water only, SG=1); (3) BHP = Q·ΔP/1714/η (PSI, works for any fluid). The constants 3960 and 1714 differ because 3960/1714 = 2.31 (PSI-to-feet conversion). Never include SG in the PSI formula; always include it in the head formula when fluid ≠ water.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: The big takeaway is that if you want to use Q·ΔH/3960 and exclude the specific gravity, it has to be water. If it's not water, you have to include the specific gravity. Why? Because the height of a column of fluid that isn't water will exert different pressure.

key_points: Unit consistency is non-negotiable: Pick pressure units (PSI) or head units (feet), then commit. Mixing approaches causes most errors.
Specific gravity is your guide for which formula to use: If working in feet of non-water fluid, you MUST include SG. If in PSI, never include it.
The three power equations are mathematically identical, but each requires different unit setup. Master one; understand why the others work.
problems.solution_video:
• **The PSI method is universal**: Q × ΔP / 1714 works for any fluid (water, fuel oil, etc.) as long as you have GPM and PSI. No specific gravity term needed.

• **Three equivalent approaches exist**: (1) PSI method (Q·ΔP/1714) — preferred, simplest; (2) Feet-of-water method (Q·ΔH/3960) — only for water; (3) Feet-of-fluid method (Q·ΔH·SG/(3960·η)) — requires unit conversions.

• **Common trap: Double-counting specific gravity**: If you use feet of head, don't multiply the 10 ft column by SG AND include SG in the formula. Pick one approach and commit.

• **Pressure unit conversions are critical**: −2.0 inHg is NOT −2.0 feet of water. Use the chain: inHg → inH₂O (×13.6) → ftH₂O (÷12) → PSI (÷2.31). Each step matters.

• **Efficiency always divides (never multiplies)**: BHP = WHP / η because the pump loses power to inefficiency. Input > Output. Many students multiply by 0.9 and get wrong answer.

• **Elevation affects pressure only if you're tracking head**: In the PSI method, the discharge gauge already reads 30 psig—elevation is already in that reading. Just add the 10 ft pressure column separately.

problems.solution: the low cost of o(cid:27)-peak electricity of $0.02 per kWh. 400gpm of 60◦F water is pumped from a low reservoir to a high reservoir at 1000ft higher elevation through an equivalent length of 3000ft of 5in schedule 40 steel pipe using a pump driven by an AC motor. A. $2 B. $13 C. $17 D. $52 The cost depends on the run time, the unit rate for electricity, and the electrical power demand, the latter which is unknown. The electrical power, P, depends on the hydraulic horsepower, whp, which can be determined using the volume (cid:29)ow rate, head added by the pump, and the e(cid:30)ciency. Note the e(cid:30)ciency given is the total e(cid:30)ciency including the pump losses and motor losses, yielding electrical power directly from hydraulic horsepower, skipping the intermediate step of specifying the brake horsepower, which is not a required step. whp Q ∆h [gpm] [ft] P = = η 3960η Write the form of the Bernoulli Equation suited for pumping applications: P −P v2−v2 ∆h=h = 2 1 + 2 1 +z −z +h A γ 2g 2 1 f Although the two reservoirs are at di(cid:27)erent elevations, presumably both are exposed to atmo- spheric pressure, and the column of air associated with the elevation di(cid:27)erence does not have a meaningful impact on the static pressure. Therefore: P −P P ≈P → 2 1 ≈0 1 2 γ Since the volume of the reservoirs is large compared to the entering (cid:29)ow, the velocity term is also negligible: v ≈v ≈0 1 2 v2−v2 2 1 ≈0 2g The di(cid:27)erence in height is given: ∆z =z −z =1000ft 2 1 212 To (cid:28)nd the losses, write the Darcy Equation: fLv2 h = f 2gD Using the Steel Pipe Friction Tables for 400gpm (cid:29)owing through a nominal 5inch standard weight steel pipe. problems.solution: spheric pressure. The static suction lift is 5ft, the static discharge head is 30ft, and the working pressure at the outlet is 10psig. The pump e(cid:30)ciency is 85% and the motor e(cid:30)ciency is 80%. Assuming losses are insigni(cid:28)cant, what size motor is required? A. 11/2hp B. 2hp C. 3hp D. 5hp Write the form of the Bernoulli Equation suited for pumping applications: P −P v2−v2 h = 2 1 + 2 1 +z −z +h A γ 2g 2 1 f The working pressure at the outlet, P , is given. The static pressure in the open reservoir,P , is 2 1 atmospheric pressure. Therefore, the gauge pressure at P is the di(cid:27)erential pressure. Use the rule 2 ofthumbconversionfactorforwater2.31ft forconvenienceconvertingbetweenpressureunitsand psi feet of head. (cid:18) (cid:19) P −P (10psi+1atm)−(1atm) 10psi ft 2 1 = = →(10psi) 2.31 =23.1ft γ γ γ psi The velocity at the outlet, v , is greater than in the reservoir, v ≈ 0;v > v . However, the 2 1 2 1 velocity term remains negligible. Feel free to include and show this to be the case. v2−v2 2 1 ≈0 2g The height di(cid:27)erential is the sum of both the static suction lift and the discharge head. ∆z =z −z =30ft+5ft=35ft 2 1 The problem states losses are insigni(cid:28)cant. Therefore: h =0 f The head added by the pump is then: h =23.1ft+35ft=58.1ft A The motor sizing is based on the bhp; therefore, the motor e(cid:30)ciency is not needed. The brake hp depends on the pump e(cid:30)ciency and hydraulic horsepower, whp, which is a function of volume (cid:29)ow rate and head added by the pump. Solve for bhp and select the smallest su(cid:30)cient motor size: whp Q ∆h (100)(58.1) [gpm] [ft] bhp= = = =1.73hp η 3960·η (3960)(.85) p p Answer B 214

problems.solution: a storage tank held at atmospheric pressure from which water is supplied. The suction piping is made up of 50ft equivalent length of 2in schedule 40 steel. The stored water is never warmer than 60◦F. What is the net positive suction head available? A. 0ft B. 16ft C. 28ft D. 46ft Start by looking up net positive suction head available in the Reference Handbook and stating the formula: NPSH =h +h −h −h A p z vpa f where h is the atmospheric pressure at the source reservoir surface, h is the height of the p z (cid:29)uid column on the suction inlet, h is the vapor pressure at the temperature of the (cid:29)uid, and vpa h is the friction loss from the (cid:29)uid source to the pump inlet. Note this formula is best suited for f design purposes. After a system is installed and (cid:28)tted with gauges and sensors, it may be suitable to use the existing conditions formula instead. In this case, there is no known pressure gauge on thesuctionside, thereforethebestformulaistheoneselected. Forconsistency, alltermsshouldbe converted to units of ft. Convert h to units of ft using the rule of thumb conversion factor for water 2.31ft. p psi (cid:18) (cid:19) ft h =14.7psi 2.31 =33.96ft p psi Sincethepumpislocatedbelowthetopofthestoragetank,h hasapositivevalue. The vapor pressure reduces the NPSH , A thus is it subtracted in the formula. During a pump failure, what brake horsepower is required to drive each of the two running pumps?

problems.statement: 100,000gpm of water falls 900ft through a hydroelectric turbine sys- tem with 90% effciency. The friction loss through the system is 100ft. How much power is generated by the system?
problems.solution: system with 90% e(cid:30)ciency. The friction loss through the system is 100ft. How much power is generated by the system? A. 13MW B. 15MW C. 17MW D. 19MW A hydroelectric turbine is e(cid:27)ectively a pump acting in reverse, turning hydraulic horsepower into brake horsepower and then turning a generator, which is e(cid:27)ectively a motor operating in reverse, turning brake horsepower into electricity. This problem gives the e(cid:30)ciency of the hydroelectric turbinesystem, thereforeitisnotnecessarytoanalyzethedetailedinteractionbetweentheturbine and the generator. Rather, the entire system can be viewed as taking water horsepower, whp, as an input, and producing electrical power, P , as an output. elec Write the form of the Bernoulli Equation suited for pumping applications: P −P v2−v2 h = 2 1 + 2 1 +z −z +h A γ 2g 2 1 f wherestate1islocatedatthesourceontheturbineinletside,state2representstheoutletside, and h is the head added to the turbine (rather than by a pump). A Both the inlet and outlet states are at atmospheric pressie. There is no need to account for the 900ft of elevation di(cid:27)erential from a static pressure perspective. Therefore: P ≈P ≈1atm 1 2 P −P 2 1 ≈0 γ The velocity term may also be neglected as the velocities are likely to be close in magnitude at the two locations. 218 v ≈v 1 2 v2−v2 2 1 ≈0 2g The height di(cid:27)erential is given. Note the sign is negative due to how to states 1 and 2 were de(cid:28)ned, i.e. state 2 is at the lower height: ∆z =z −z =0ft−900ft=−900ft 2 1 The friction loss is given: h =100ft f Calculate the head pressure removed by the turbine. Note this value is expected to be negative since a positive value would correspond to the head being added, as would occur using a pump. In the nextstep when calculatingthe water horsepower, it is perfectly acceptableto take the absolute value. P −P v2−v2 h = 2 1 + 2 1 +z −z +h =−900ft+100ft=−800ft A γ 2g 2 1 f The electrical power produced is a portion of the whp, as de(cid:28)ne

problems.solution: temper the fresh air intake during winter operation. The heating coil is sized for 80,000Btu. The outside conditions are 20◦F and 50% hr relative humidity. What is the humidity ratio after heating? As- sume that the degree of saturation is roughly equivalent to relative humidity. A. 0.001lbw lbda B. 0.002lbw lbda C. 0.003lbw lbda D. 0.004lbw lbda The humidity ratio after heating is the same as the humidity ratio before heating because the heating process is purely sensible only, i.e. there is no moisture being added or removed by the heating coil. Therefore, the problem can be distilled down to (cid:28)nding the humidity ratio of the outside air based on known temperature and relative humidity. Although the state is fully de(cid:28)ned, the psychrometric chart only goes down to 35◦F and no low temperature psychrometric chart is provided in the Reference Handbook. As an alternative, search for the table Thermodynamic Properties of Moist Air and note the humidity ratio at saturation (100% relative humidity): lb @T =20◦F →ω =0.002153 w s lb da 222 The humidity ratio at any other level of humidity can be calculated by using the degree of saturation, µ. As an approximation, it is acceptable to assume the degree of saturation is roughly equivalent to the relative humidity. This is not strictly true but works well in a pinch. Solve for the humidity ratio using µ. φ≈µ≈50% ω µ= → ω =µω ω s s (cid:18) (cid:19) lb lb ω =(.5) 0.002153 w =0.00108 w lb lb da da Answer A

To specify the change in enthalpy, use the psychrometric chart to look up the enthalpy values for entering and leaving conditions, both of which are fully de(cid:28)ned. problems.solution: freezer. How much energy is removed? A. 150Btu B. 440Btu C. 1460Btu D. 1480Btu The total energy removed is the sum of the sensible heat removed above the freezing point, latent heat removed at the freezing point, and sensible heat removed below the freezing point. In the Reference Handbook, look up speci(cid:28)c heats of foods and (cid:28)nd pork. Note that the freezing point of pork is 28◦F. The speci(cid:28)c heats above and below freezing are also given, as well as the latent heat of fusion. Start by writing the expression for sensible heat removed above the freezing point: q =mc ∆T s,above p,above Write an expression for the latent heat removed: q =mh l fg Write an expression for the sensible heat removed below the freezing point: q =mc ∆T s,below p,below The total heat removed is the sum of these three expressions. For convenience, factor out the mass which is common to all terms. Substitute and solve for the total heat removed in Btu: q =q +q +q =mc ∆T +mh +mc ∆T t s,above l s,below p,above fg p,below q =m[c ∆T +h +c ∆T] t p,above fg p,below (cid:20)(cid:18) (cid:19) (cid:18) (cid:19) (cid:18) (cid:19) (cid:21) Btu Btu Btu q =(10lb) .86 (68◦F −28◦F)+ 104 + .53 (28◦F −10◦F) t lb◦F lb lb◦F q =1479Btu t Answer D 240

problems.statement: An uninsulated 8in chilled water pipe carries 55◦F chilled water through a factory maintained at 80◦F. The length of the run is 75ft. The pipe is painted with nonmetallic paint. What is the net heat transfer by radiation? hr hr hr hr
problems.solution: through a factory maintained at 80◦F. The length of the run is 75ft. The pipe is painted with nonmetallic paint. What is the net heat transfer by radiation? A. 3500Btu hr B. 4000Btu hr C. 40,000Btu hr D. 120,000Btu hr It is not unreasonable to expect there to be some heat transfer by convection in the scenario described; however, the question states that only radiation should be considered. Treat the chilled water pipe as a body that is small compared to its surroundings and select the classic radiation equation: Q˙ =εσA (cid:0) T4−T4(cid:1) 1 2 245 whereQisheattransfer,εistheemissivity,σ istheStefan-Boltzmannconstant,Aisthesurface area, and T is for the respective temperatures of the body and the surroundings. Treating the pipe as a cylinder (with no ends), the surface area is: (cid:18) (cid:19) 1ft A=πDL=π(8in) (75ft)=157ft2 12in When calculating radiation, absolute temperature units are required. Therefore: T =80◦F +460=540◦R 1 T =55◦F +460=515◦R 2 To (cid:28)nd the emissivity, (cid:28)nd the table in the Reference Handbook for Emissivity of Surfaces and take note of the average emissivity for nonmetallic paints: ε=.90 The Stefan-Boltzmann constant can be found in the table Fundamental Constants: Btu σ =0.1713×10−8 hr·ft2·R4 Solve for the net heat transfer: Q˙ =εσA (cid:0) T4−T4(cid:1) 1 2 Q˙ =(.9) (cid:18) 0.1713×10−8 Btu (cid:19) (cid:0) 157ft2(cid:1)(cid:104) (540◦R)4−(515◦R)4 (cid:105) =3560 Btu hr·ft2·R4 hr Answer A 246

problems.statement: A 75KVA transformer with 99% effciency is located in an electrical room at 70◦F. The transformer’s enclosure is a floor standing box with dimensions of 3ft height, 2ft width, 3ft depth. The exposed surfaces have an average temperature of 85◦F. The load is resistive and uses 60% of the transformer’s capacity. What is the overall heat transfer coeffcient? hr·ft2·◦F hr·ft2·◦F hr·ft2·◦F hr·ft2·◦F 39
problems.solution: room at 70◦F. The transformer’s enclosure is a (cid:29)oor standing box with dimensions of 3ft height, 2ft width, 3ft depth. The exposed surfaces have an average temperature of 85◦F. The load is resistive and uses 60% of the transformer’s capacity. What is the overall heat transfer coe(cid:30)cient? A. 1.9 Btu hr·ft2·◦F B. 2.4 Btu hr·ft2·◦F C. 2.8 Btu hr·ft2·◦F D. 4.7 Btu hr·ft2·◦F The Overall Heat Transfer Coe(cid:30)cient is given by: Q˙ Q˙ =UA∆T →U = A∆T The surface area for the (cid:29)oor standing enclosure includes 5 faces: the top and 4 sides. Note there is no base area to include in the surface area: A=(2)[(3ft)(3ft)]+(3)[(2ft)(3ft)]=36ft2 The temperature di(cid:27)erential is the di(cid:27)erence between the exposed surfaces of the enclosure and the ambient temperature in the electrical room: ∆T =85◦F −70◦F =15◦F The heat produced by the transformer depends on the demand, e(cid:30)ciency, and power factor. The load is purely resistive; therefore the power factor should be taken as unity: KW PF = =1 KVA The real power draw i.e. the heat load in KW is then: (cid:18) (cid:19) 1KW P =(75KVA)(.6) =45KW 1KVA Calculate the losses associated with a 99% e(cid:30)cient transformer, converted to Btu: (cid:18) (cid:19) Btu Btu Q˙ =(45KW)(.01) 3412 =1535 hr·KW hr Calculate U: Q˙ (cid:0) 1535Btu(cid:1) Btu U = = hr =2.84 A∆T (36ft2)(15◦F) hr·ft2·◦F Answer C 247

problems.statement: 48(cid:176)F chilled water flows in a 200ft long 8in steel pipe with 2in of insulation through a room maintained at 73◦F. The insulation has a thermal conductivity of 1.8 Btu·in . The overall coeffcient of heat hr·ft2·◦F transfer for the outside of the insulation to the surrounding room including convection and radiation is 2 Btu . What is the surface hr·ft2·◦F temperature of the outside of the insulation?
problems.solution: insulation through a room maintained at 73◦F. The insulation has a thermal conductivity of 1.8 Btu·in . The overall coe(cid:30)cient of heat hr·ft2·◦F transfer for the outside of the insulation to the surrounding room including convection and radiation is 2 Btu . What is the surface hr·ft2·◦F temperature of the outside of the insulation? A. 55.8◦F B. 59.8◦F C. 61.2◦F D. 66.3◦F Heatisgainedfromtheambientspaceandenterstheinsulationaccordingtoanoverallheattransfer coe(cid:30)cient for the insulation (which includes both convection and radiation). The same quantity of heat then propagates through the insulation by conduction based on the thickness and thermal conductivity of the insulation. Heat travels through the thickness of the pipe by conduction, and (cid:28)nally, into the (cid:29)owing water by convection. Neglect the heat transfer by conduction through the thickness of the pipe because the steel is highly conductive in comparison to the insulation, and no thermal conductivity was given for the pipe. Neglect the heat transfer by convection into the water as the (cid:29)ow is likely to be turbulent and the chilled water temperature will quickly reach equilibrium with the pipe. Therefore, consider only the heat gain by the insulation from the room by convection, and the heat gain by conduction through the insulation. These quantities must be equal. Write an expression for the heat gain by the insulation from the room as though it were a composite plane wall: Q =U A∆T =U A(T −T ) conv,rad o o room surface Writeanexpressionfortheheatgainbyconductionthroughtheinsulationbasedontheformula for conduction through a cylindrical wall: k Q =2πL (T −T ) cond (cid:16) (cid:17) surface water ln r2 r1 Setthesetwoexpressionsequal. Recognizeareaisthesurfaceareaoftheoutsideoftheinsulation where the conduction and radiation are acting on the insulation, which is given by 2πr L, where 2 2πL cancels from both sides leaving r on the left. 2 Q =Q conv,rad cond k U A(T −T )=2πL (T −T ) o

problems.solution: water using a parallel (cid:29)ow shell and tube heat exchanger. Low temperature hot water from a boiler enters the heat exchanger at 160◦F and leaves at 130◦F. What is the log mean temperature di(cid:27)erence? The smallest temperature di(cid:27)erential between the two streams will be observed at the outlet and the highest temperature di(cid:27)erential will be observed at the inlet. Call the inlet temperature di(cid:27)erential ∆T and the outlet temperature di(cid:27)erential ∆T : A B ∆T =160◦F −55◦F =105◦F A 258 ∆T =130◦F −120◦F =10◦F B Recall the simpli(cid:28)ed LMTD equation o(cid:27)ered in solution 10.2. problems.statement: In the summer, a warehouse is maintained at 72◦F by several chilled beams supplied with 58◦F chilled water which returns at 65◦F. Treating the chilled beam coil as a heat exchanger, what is the log mean temperature di(cid:27)erence?
problems.solution: chilled beams supplied with 58◦F chilled water which returns at 65◦F. Treating the chilled beam coil as a heat exchanger, what is the log mean temperature di(cid:27)erence? A. 7◦F B. 10◦F C. 11◦F D. 14◦F Totreatthechilledbeamcoilasaheatexchanger, considerthechilledwatertobethecoldstream, (cid:176) (cid:176) entering at 58 F and leaving at 65 F. The air side will represent the warm stream, however, we do not know the supply and return temperature. We could assume the return temperature is representative of the room and guess that the supply air temperature is somewhat lower. This would not be unreasonable, although the supply air temperature chosen would be an outright guess. Thinking about the way a ceiling mounted chilled beam works, in particular the passive style, thestrati(cid:28)cationoftheairnaturallybringswarmairintocontactwiththechilledbeam,whichcools the air which gently falls away, promoting mixing vertically throughout the space without the use of a fan. Also since the chilled water is supplied at a higher temperature, there is an opportunity to capture savings not only by omitting the fan, but also by raising the chilled water setpoint for the system. As an approximation, suppose the passive chilled beam is holding the space in equilibrium, and the supply and return temperatures are nearly equal. problems.solution_summary:
Step 1: Identify psychrometric states. State 1 (outside): 10°F, 50% RH. State 2 (inside): 74°F, 50% RH. The psych chart doesn't go below ~32°F, so use the "Thermodynamic Properties of Moist Air" table below it.

Step 2: Find humidity ratios. From the table: ω₁ ≈ 0.00065 lb_w/lb_da (half of saturation value at 10°F). From psych chart: ω₂ = 0.00893 lb_w/lb_da.

Step 3: Find specific volume at entering condition. v₁ = 11.85 ft³/lb (from the moist air table at 10°F).

Step 4: Calculate mass flow rate of water added. ṁ_w = (V̇/v₁) × (ω₂ − ω₁) = (2000/11.85) × (0.00893 − 0.00065) = 1.4 lb_w/min.

Step 5: Convert to gallons over 24 hours. Volume = 1.4 lb/min × 60 min/hr × 24 hr × (1 ft³/62.4 lb) × (7.48 gal/ft³) = 242 gal → Answer D (240 gal).
problems.solution_full:
Both the inside and outside conditions are fully defined; however, the humidity ratio at the low temperature (outside) condition cannot be looked up on the psychrometric chart in the Reference Handbook. Use the "Thermodynamic Properties of Moist Air" table below the psych charts, or take the humidity ratio outside as approximately zero because air at 10°F can hold very little moisture.

Let the entering air condition be State 1 and the room condition be State 2.

State 1 (Outside): T₁ = 10°F, φ₁ = 50%, ω₁ = 0.00065 lb_w/lb_da ≈ 0, v₁ = 11.85 ft³/lb
State 2 (Inside): T₂ = 74°F, φ₂ = 50%, ω₂ = 0.00893 lb_w/lb_da

Find the mass flow rate of water vapor being added using:
ṁ_w = ṁ_a × (ω₂ − ω₁)

where ṁ_a = V̇/v₁ (volume flow rate ÷ specific volume).

Substituting:
ṁ_w = (V̇/v₁) × (ω₂ − ω₁)
ṁ_w = (2000 ft³/min ÷ 11.85 ft³/lb) × (0.00893 − 0.00065) lb_w/lb_da
ṁ_w = 168.8 lb_a/min × 0.00828 lb_w/lb_da = 1.4 lb_w/min

Convert to gallons over 24 hours:
1.4 lb/min × 60 min/hr × 24 hr × (1 ft³/62.4 lb) × (7.48 gal/ft³) = 242 gal

Answer D (240 gal).

Note: If the humidity ratio at State 1 is taken as zero, the result is overstated by about 7%, but still leads to the correct answer choice.
problems.solution_video:
• **The psych chart doesn't go low enough**: At 10°F, you're well below the ~32°F lower limit of the Reference Handbook's psychrometric chart. Don't panic — use the "Thermodynamic Properties of Moist Air" table just a few pages below.

• **Three ways to handle the low-temperature humidity ratio**: (1) Use the moist air table — find ω at saturation, take 50% of it. (2) Extrapolate the psych chart — possible but risky and imprecise. (3) Assume ω₁ ≈ 0 — cold air holds almost no moisture, so it's a rounding error. You'll overstate humidification by ~7%, still get the right answer.

• **The key formula is ṁ_w = ṁ_a × (ω₂ − ω₁)**: Mass flow rate of water = mass flow rate of air × change in humidity ratio. This comes from the definition of humidity ratio and should be memorized.

• **Convert volume flow to mass flow using specific volume**: ṁ_a = V̇ / v₁. Use the specific volume at the entering (outdoor) condition — that's where you know the volume flow rate.

• **Unit conversion chain matters**: lb/min → lb/day (×60×24) → ft³/day (÷62.4) → gal/day (×7.48). Each step is straightforward but missing one gives a wrong answer.

• **Common trap: Using the wrong formula entirely**: Some students reach for 1.08·CFM·ΔT (sensible heat rule of thumb). That's for sensible heating/cooling, NOT humidification. This problem is about adding moisture, not temperature change.

oh_questions_full.theme: Finding humidity ratio at very low temperatures — the #1 question on this problem
oh_questions_full.question: The psychrometric chart only goes down to about 32°F. How do I find the humidity ratio at 10°F and 50% RH?
oh_questions_full.answer: This comes up on almost every office hours where HVAC-4 is discussed. The psych chart in the Reference Handbook doesn't go low enough, but there's a table called "Thermodynamic Properties of Moist Air" just a few pages below it. That table gives the humidity ratio at saturation (100% RH) for low temperatures. For 50% RH, take half of the saturation value — it's not technically exact, but it's more than close enough. The saturation humidity ratio at 10°F is about 0.0013, so at 50% RH you'd use 0.00065. Alternatively, you can just assume ω₁ ≈ 0 because cold air holds almost no moisture. You'll overstate the humidification requirement by about 7%, but you'll still land on the correct answer choice.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: If the air is that cold, then it can barely hold any moisture at all. And it's only holding 50% of what it could if it was saturated. So we can just say that it's a rounding error.

oh_questions_full.theme: Which specific volume to use — entering vs. leaving conditions
oh_questions_full.question: How do you determine which specific volume of air to use? I used the indoor condition initially and got the wrong answer.
oh_questions_full.answer: Always use the specific volume that corresponds to where you know the volume flow rate. In this problem, the 2000 CFM is the outside air entering the unit, so use the specific volume at the outdoor (entering) condition — v₁ = 11.85 ft³/lb. If you use the indoor specific volume (~13.5 ft³/lb), you'll underestimate the mass flow rate of air and get a lower answer (~209 gal instead of 242). The reason: you're converting volume flow to mass flow, and the density/specific volume must match the location where the volume flow rate is measured. When in doubt, use the entering condition — it works 90-95% of the time.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: Always use the entering conditions. The sooner you kind of just adopt this method of always using the entering conditions for the specific volume, this is a problem that will go away and things will be very smooth for you.

oh_questions_full.theme: Using the wrong formula — sensible heat rule of thumb instead of humidification
oh_questions_full.question: I used Q_s = 1.08 × CFM × ΔT and got 103 gallons. What does that number actually represent?
oh_questions_full.answer: The 1.08·CFM·ΔT formula is for sensible heating or cooling — it tells you the rate of temperature change, not moisture addition. This problem is about humidification (adding water to air), which is a latent process. There's no reason to use the sensible heat rule of thumb here. The correct approach is ṁ_w = ṁ_a × (ω₂ − ω₁), which directly calculates moisture addition from the humidity ratio difference. The broader lesson: before diving into equations, take a moment to frame what the question is actually asking. Road-map the problem first, then pick the right tool.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: There's no reason to reach for 1.08·CFM·ΔT. The problem was about finding how much water is needed for humidification. Don't be too hasty to reach for an equation and start down a path.

oh_questions_full.theme: Using the reference handbook humidification equation (Section 9.3.10) and getting a different answer
oh_questions_full.question: I used the humidification load equation from Section 9.3.10 and got 209 gallons instead of 240. Why the discrepancy?
oh_questions_full.answer: The Section 9.3.10 equation (H = 60 × ρ × CFM × Δω) is perfectly valid — it's algebraically equivalent to the approach in the solution video. The discrepancy came from using ρ = 0.074 lb/ft³ (which corresponds to a specific volume of ~13.5 ft³/lb — the indoor condition) instead of the entering outdoor air density. With v₁ = 11.85 ft³/lb (ρ ≈ 0.084 lb/ft³), you'd get the same 242 gallons. Both methods work; the key is using the correct density/specific volume for the location where you know the volume flow rate.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: I have no objection to that equation. I did it a little bit differently because I just think about it on a more intuitive level. I'm always hesitant to just go for a plug-in-chug equation.

oh_questions_full.theme: Finding specific volume at low temperature — the moist air table has two values
oh_questions_full.question: The table gives specific volume for dry air and saturated air at 10°F. Which one do I use?
oh_questions_full.answer: The table gives specific volume for dry air (0% RH) and saturated air (100% RH) at each temperature. At very low temperatures, the difference between these two values is minuscule — around 0.025 ft³/lb. For 50% RH, you could interpolate halfway between them, but honestly, just pick either one. Dan used 11.85 ft³/lb in the solution. The precision difference is negligible and won't affect your answer choice.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: They're separated by a minuscule amount. If you want to take the average, be my guest. If you want to just eyeball that and pick something in the middle, you're going to be okay either way.

oh_questions_full.theme: Understanding the humidity ratio formula — why ṁ_w = ṁ_a × Δω works
oh_questions_full.question: Can you explain why the mass flow rate of water equals mass flow rate of air times the change in humidity ratio?
oh_questions_full.answer: It comes directly from the definition of humidity ratio. Humidity ratio ω = mass of water / mass of dry air. So if you rearrange: mass of water = ω × mass of dry air. For a flow process, mass becomes mass flow rate, and the water added is the difference between the two states: ṁ_w = ṁ_a × (ω₂ − ω₁). The rates cancel when you think about it dimensionally — it's just lb_water/lb_air × lb_air/min = lb_water/min. This formula is used frequently in psychrometrics and should be memorized.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: If you divide the mass flow rate of air to both sides, now you have humidity ratio on one side. The units kind of check out — pounds of water over pounds of air. That shows why this formula is set up the way it is.

oh_questions_full.theme: Can you assume ω₁ = 0 for very cold air?
oh_questions_full.question: Is it really okay to just assume the entering humidity ratio is zero? That feels too aggressive.
oh_questions_full.answer: Yes, for very cold air (like 10°F), the humidity ratio is so small that treating it as zero introduces only about a 7% error in the final answer — which won't change your answer choice. You'll slightly overstate the humidification requirement (because Δω gets bigger), so look for an answer choice that's slightly lower than your calculation. That said, it's not hard to use the moist air table, so Dan recommends getting the actual value when possible. But in a pinch on exam day, ω₁ ≈ 0 is a perfectly defensible shortcut.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: I'm not encouraging you to just be a cowboy and do whatever you want, but you can do that and a lot of times you'll be okay.

oh_questions_full.theme: Mass flow rate of air changes during humidification — does that matter?
oh_questions_full.question: If we're adding water to the air stream, doesn't the mass flow rate change from state 1 to state 2? Should I account for that?
oh_questions_full.answer: Technically yes — ṁ_air,2 = ṁ_air,1 + ṁ_water. But the amount of water being added is tiny compared to the air mass flow. The humidity ratios are in the thousandths (0.00065 to 0.00893 lb_w/lb_da), so the water addition is less than 1% of the air mass. Using the entering condition (state 1) for the mass flow rate avoids a trial-and-error situation and the error is negligible. This is another reason to use the entering specific volume — it keeps things clean.
oh_questions_full.dan_quote: I don't want to end up in a trial and error situation where I guess what this is, calculate ṁ_a at state 2, if I'm assuming we know the volume flow rate at state 2.

key_points: Know where the low-temperature moist air table is: scroll past the three psych charts in the Reference Handbook to find "Thermodynamic Properties of Moist Air." This is essential for temperatures below ~32°F.
Always use the entering condition for specific volume when converting CFM to mass flow rate. Using the wrong state's specific volume is the most common computational error on this problem.
The core formula ṁ_w = ṁ_a × (ω₂ − ω₁) comes directly from the definition of humidity ratio. Memorize it — it appears in nearly every humidification and dehumidification problem.

problems.statement: A warehouse located in a damp climate uses two-pipe heating fan coil units to maintain a maximum relative humidity of 60%. With the fan coil units disabled and all heating control valves closed, the room reaches 62◦F and 80% RH. There are (12) (cid:214) 200cfm fan coil units. What is the required heating capacity per unit? hr hr hr hr
problems.solution: coil units to maintain a maximum relative humidity of 60%. With the fan coil units disabled and all heating control valves closed, the room reaches 62◦F and 80% RH. There are (12) (cid:214) 200cfm fan coil units. What is the required heating capacity per unit? A. 1800Btu hr B. 2300Btu hr C. 22,000Btu hr D. 28,000Btu hr The fan coil units provide heating only; therefore, they cannot remove any moisture from the air, asthatwouldrequireacoolingprocesstodrivedehumidi(cid:28)cation. Tomaintainamaximumrelative humidity, theairmustbeheated. Thismayinitiallyseemcounter-intuitive, butrecallthatwarmer air has a greater capacity for holding moisture. Su(cid:30)cient heating (without any change to the humidity ratio) will drive the relative humidity down. Drawing this on the psychrometric chart, the process line is horizontal to the right for purely sensible heating. State 1 is fully de(cid:28)ned. Obtain the dew point temperature (alternatively the humidity ratio may also be used, as both parameters will keep the process line horizontal when held constant). T =62◦F db,1 φ =80% 1 277 T =55.8◦F dp,1 For state 2, since the process line is horizontal, the dew point temperature is the same. Taken together, the dew point and the relative humidity fully de(cid:28)ne state 2. Obtain the dry bulb tem- perature at state 2 (after heating). T =T =55.8◦F dp,2 dp,1 φ =60% 2 T =70.4◦F db,2 Usethesensibleheatingruleofthumbtocalculatetherequiredheatingcapacitytoincreasethe dry bulb temperature from state 1 to state 2. Note it is not necessary to multiply by the number of fan coil units as the problem asks for the capacity per unit. Q˙ =1.08cfm∆T s Btu Q˙ =1.08(200)(70.4−62)=1814 s hr Answer A

Answer D 282

Write an expression for the quantity of heat lost during the heating season. Be sure the resulting units are in Btu. Q Q˙ = →Q=Q˙t t There is no cookie-cutter formula for this problem. Multiplying the heating degree days with Q˙ cancels time, leaving only energy and temperature. Dividing by the design ∆T eliminates temper- ature: Q= HDD×Q˙ = (5630deg·days) (cid:16) 1 2 d 4h ay r (cid:17)(cid:0) 5,000,000B h t r u(cid:1) =9.65×109Btu ∆T (70◦F −0◦F) Note that degree days account for the heat loss and ∆T being less than design for a great deal of the season. Heating systems are designed for the maximum (or near maximum) demand for the location, which may only be required on a few days per year. Calculatethecostofnaturalgas. Notetheheatingvalueisgivenin Btu andthecostisin $ , ft3 CCF where CCF = 100ft3. Also account for the e(cid:30)ciency of the system, which will drive costs higher than if the system was 100% e(cid:30)cient. (cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:16) (cid:17) 9.65×109Btu $0.90 1000Btu 100ft3 Cost= ft3 =$108,579 (.8) Answer C 297

problems.statement: The compressor for a water cooled chiller draws 40KW. The chiller delivers 100gpm with a 10◦F delta T. A cooling tower serving the chiller provides 80gpm of 80◦F condenser water. What is the tem- perature of the condenser water leaving the chiller? 61
problems.solution: delivers 100gpm with a 10◦F delta T. A cooling tower serving the chiller provides 80gpm of 80◦F condenser water. What is the tem- perature of the condenser water leaving the chiller? A. 74◦F B. 78◦F C. 93◦F D. 96◦F Use the sensible heating/cooling rule of thumb for water to determine the total load on the evapo- rator, which corresponds to the water delivered by the chiller: Btu Q˙ =500gpm∆T =500(100)(10)=500,000 in hr Determine the total heat to be rejected by the condenser, which is the sum of the evaporator load and the compressor power. Be sure to align units. Q˙ =Q˙ +W˙ out in in (cid:18) (cid:19) Btu Btu Btu Q˙ =500,000 +40KW 3412 =636,480 out hr hr·KW hr Apply the sensible heating/cooling rule of thumb for water again, this time for the condenser, to determine the condenser water return temperature. Note the naming may be counter-intuitive since the water leaving the chiller’s condenser is returning to the cooling tower. The nomenclature for condenser water is typically considered from the perspective of the cooling tower. Q˙ =500gpm∆T =500gpm(CWR−CWS) out Btu Q˙ =500(80)(CWR−80◦F)=636,480 out hr CWR−80◦F =15.9◦F CWR=95.9◦F Answer D 312

problems.solution: at the perimeter of a building and experiences excessive in(cid:28)ltration from the outdoors amounting to 2ACH. What is the total cooling demand? A. 2tons B. 13tons C. 14tons D. 27tons To determine the in(cid:28)ltration load, use the Psychrometric Chart to obtain the enthalpy for the outside air and the enthalpy for the internal space, both of which are fully de(cid:28)ned. For the outside air, State 1: T =95◦F 1 φ =80% 1 Btu h =54.83 1 lb For the internal space, State 2: T =72◦F 2 φ =50◦F 2 Btu h =26.43 2 lb Calculate the change in enthalpy required to condition the outside air: Btu Btu ∆h=h −h =54.83 −26.43 1 2 lb lb Based on the ACH and dimensions of the space, (cid:28)nd the volume (cid:29)ow rate for the in(cid:28)ltration: (cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19) V˙ = (cid:0) 3000ft2(cid:1) (12ft) 2airchanges 1hr =1200cfm hr 60min Use the total heating/cooling rule of thumb for air to determine the cooling load due to in(cid:28)l- tration: Q˙ =4.5cfm∆h infiltration 313 Btu Q˙ =4.5(1200)(28.4)=153,360 infiltration hr Determine the total cooling load including the internal heat load as well as the in(cid:28)ltration. Align units to Btu, then convert to refrigeration tons: hr Q˙ =Q˙ +Q˙ total infiltration internal (cid:18) (cid:19) Btu Btu Btu Q˙ =153,360 +(50KW) 3412 =323,960 total hr hr·KW hr (cid:32) (cid:33) Btu 1ton Q˙ =323,960 =27tons total hr 12000Btu hr Answer D

problems.statement: A pumping system delivers water to a factory through a standard weight steel piping supply line (surface roughness C = 120) with 3 outlets delivering 100gpm to each outlet. The main pipe initially has a 5in nominal diameter, reducing to 4in and 3in after each branch outlet. The first outlet is located 50ft from the pumping station; the second outlet is 100ft downstream of the first, and the third outlet is 100ft downstream of the second. What is the pressure loss for the system? Ignore minor losses.
problems.solution: weight steel piping supply line (surface roughness C = 120) with 3 outlets delivering 100gpm to each outlet. The main pipe initially has a 5in nominal diameter, reducing to 4in and 3in after each branch outlet. The (cid:28)rst outlet is located 50ft from the pumping station; the second outlet is 100ft downstream of the (cid:28)rst, and the third outlet is 100ft downstream of the second. What is the pressure loss for the system? Ignore minor losses. A. 7ft B. 10ft C. 14ft D. 20ft Thepressurelossisbasedonthe(cid:29)owrate, diameter, surfaceroughness, andthelengthofthepipe. Break the problem into 3 sections and use the Steel Pipe Friction Tables to look up the head loss per 100 ft for each section. The surface roughness may be considered at the end. For the 5 inch section: D =5in L=50ft Q=300gpm h =3ft/100ft d.loss 314 (cid:18) (cid:19) 3ft h =(50ft) f 100ft For the 4 inch section, the (cid:29)ow is reduced by 100gpm after the (cid:28)rst branch: D =4in L=100ft Q=200gpm h =4.3ft/100ft d.loss (cid:18) (cid:19) 4.3ft h =(100ft) f 100ft For the 3 inch section, the (cid:29)ow is reduced again by 100gpm after the second branch: D =3in L=100ft Q=100gpm h =4.5ft/100ft d.loss (cid:18) (cid:19) 4.5ft h =(100ft) f 100ft Look up Surface Roughness Factors in the Reference Handbook under the Steel Pipe Friction Tables and refer to the correction factors. For a surface roughness of C = 120, it is necessary to multiply the total head loss by .71. Take the sum of the losses from the 3 sections and apply the correction factor to determine the total pressure loss for the system: (cid:20) (cid:18) (cid:19) (cid:18) (cid:19) (cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:21) 3ft 4.3ft 4.5ft h =(.71) (50ft) +(100ft) +(100ft) =7.3 f 100ft 100ft 100ft Answer A 315

problems.solution: heat exchanger. The shower heads are rated for 5gpm each. Ex- pected demand can be estimated as 25% of maximum. Cold water enters the heat exchanger at 55◦F and leaves at 115◦F. 500lb of hr 5psig steam is supplied to the heat exchanger and leaves saturated. What is the e(cid:30)ciency of the heat exchanger? A. 90% B. 92% C. 94% D. 96% The heat transferred to the water is less than the heat given up by the steam due to losses. The e(cid:30)ciency is determined by calculating both quantities. Forthewatersideoftheheatexchanger,usethesensibleheatingruleofthumbforwaterto(cid:28)nd the heat added. Before solving, apply the diversity in the (cid:29)ow rate demand. It is not necessary to size the heat exchanger for all 12 shower heads to supply the full 5gpm simultaneously. Make sure to convert psig to psia before obtaining values. problems.statement: Due to acoustical considerations, the compressor and condenser for a 2ton refrigerator with a COP of 5 are located remotely in a nearby storage closet. Neglecting lights and other ancillary heat sources, what is the required cooling capacity for the storage closet? hr hr hr hr
problems.solution: a 2ton refrigerator with a COP of 5 are located remotely in a nearby storage closet. Neglecting lights and other ancillary heat sources, what is the required cooling capacity for the storage closet? A. 24,000Btu hr B. 25,000Btu hr C. 29,000Btu hr D. 33,000Btu hr Thecoolingsystemfortheclosetneedstobeabletosupportthefullheatrejectionfromthesystem, which includes the compressor and the condenser. As with all refrigeration cycles, the compressor energyisaddedtotherefrigerant,thereforetheheatrejectionatthecondenserisalreadyexhaustive, with the possible minor exception of local heat loss from the compressor due to any ine(cid:30)ciency. However, even in that case, any local heat generation is being added to the room directly rather than the refrigerant loop, and will no longer need to be rejected from the condenser. Ultimately, the sum of the heat generated, and thus the cooling required for the room, is nothing more or less than Q for the cycle. out ApplytheCoe(cid:30)cientofPerformance(COP)forarefrigeratortocalculatethecompressorpower: Q˙ Q˙ 2tons COP = in →W˙ = in = =.4tons R W˙ in in COP R 5 Calculate the heat rejection from the condenser, and convert to Btu: hr Q˙ =Q˙ +W˙ =2tons+.4tons=2.4tons out in in (cid:32) (cid:33) 12,000Btu Btu Q˙ =2.4tons hr =28,800 out ton hr Answer C 317

What is the log mean temperature di(cid:27)erence? A. 62◦F B. 68◦F C. 76◦F D. 90◦F For a counter(cid:29)ow heat exchanger, the hot stream enters on the opposite side from the cold stream suchthatthe∆T alongthelengthisclosetotheaverage∆T. (Contrastthiswiththetemperature pro(cid:28)leforaparallel(cid:29)owheatexchangerwhichvariesdramaticallyalongthelength.) Arbitrarilycall thehotenteringend(cid:16)SideA(cid:17) andcoldenteringend(cid:16)SideB.(cid:17) Calculatethetemperaturedi(cid:27)erences at both ends. It may be useful to draw the temperature pro(cid:28)le on a Temperature vs. Length diagram. ∆T =160◦F −70◦F =90◦F A ∆T =100◦F −50◦F =50◦F B Look up Log Mean Temperature Di(cid:27)erence (LMTD) in the Reference Handbook. Consider applying the simpli(cid:28)ed version of this formula initially introduced during in the Heat Transfer section of this book: ∆T −∆T 90◦F −50◦F LMTD = A B = =68◦F ln (cid:16) ∆ ∆ T T B A (cid:17) ln (cid:0)9 5 0 0 ◦ ◦ F F (cid:1) Answer B 318

problems.solution: 13ft above the centerline of the associated 50gpm condenser wa- ter pump. The entering condenser water for the tower is 92◦F. Consider each term one at a time. Atmospheric pressure is readily known to be 14.7psia. For consistency, convert units to ft of head by applying by the rule of thumb conversion factor for water 2.31ft=1psi. (cid:18) (cid:19) 2.31ft h =14.7psi =33.96ft p psi The height of the water column on the suction side is given: h =13ft z The vapor pressure of water is a function of temperature. The warmer the water, the higher the vapor pressure because more water molecules are likely to undergo phase change and become vapor. The average outdoor conditions for the region are 88◦F and 55% rela- tive humidity. The unit manufacturer o(cid:27)ers an option to include an enthalpy wheel with 60% e(cid:27)ectiveness for an additional cost of $10,000. If it costs the tenant $0.50 per ton hour to produce chilled water, what is the simple payback for the enthalpy wheel? Neglect maintenance and assume installation and commissioning add no additional upfront cost. 70
problems.solution: for a 24 hour gym which is to be maintained at 72◦F with 50% relative humidity by supplying 55◦F saturated supply air. The average outdoor conditions for the region are 88◦F and 55% rela- tive humidity. The unit manufacturer o(cid:27)ers an option to include an enthalpy wheel with 60% e(cid:27)ectiveness for an additional cost of $10,000. If it costs the tenant $0.50 per ton hour to produce chilled water, what is the simple payback for the enthalpy wheel? Neglect maintenance and assume installation and commissioning add no additional upfront cost. A. 3 months B. 10 months C. 2 years D. 3 years An enthalpy wheel is capable of recovering both sensible and latent heat. Contrast this with an air-to-air heat exchanger which captures only sensible heat. The enthalpy wheel provides the opportunity to cool and dehumify the entering outside air by rejecting heat and moisture to the room air being exhausted. If the enthalpy wheel had 100% e(cid:27)ectivenss, the enthalpy of the air supplied by the enthalpy wheel would be equal to the enthalpy of the room air. Further cooling anddehum(cid:28)cationwouldalwayshavetobeprovidedbytheoutsideairhandlingunit’scoolingcoil. Therefore, the e(cid:27)ectiveness of the enthalpy wheel, and thus the opportunity to produce savings, applies only to the enthalpy di(cid:27)erential between the outside air and the room air. The OAHU discharge conditions are to be ignored. ConsidertheoutsideairasState1andtheroomairasState2. UsethePsychrometricChartto look up the enthalpy for both states. Use the total cooling rule of thumb to calculate the amount of energy removed by the enthalpy wheel if it was 100% e(cid:27)ective, and take 60% of that value to account for the given e(cid:27)ectiveness. T =88◦F 1 φ =55% 1 Btu h =38.4 1 lb T =72◦F 2 φ =50% 2 Btu h =26.4 2 lb 343 Q˙ =4.5cfm∆h t Btu Q˙ =(0.6)(4.5)(1000)(38.4−26.4)=32,400 t hr This is the total avoided cooling load. Convert this value to refrigerationtons and calculate the annual ton·hours: (cid:18)

problems.solution: rated suction temperature of −50◦F and a discharge temperature of 10◦F. The discharge line is a 13/ 8 in type L copper tube with a length of 100ft. What is the capacity of the discharge line? A. 4.5tons B. 5.6tons C. 6.6tons D. 7.9tons Look up Discharge and Liquid Line Capacities in the Reference Handbook and (cid:28)nd the table for Refrigerant 22, speci(cid:28)cally Intermediate or Low-Stage Duty. The suction temperature is extra information and is not needed for determining the capacity of the discharge line. Based on Type L Copper tube with the diameter given, obtain the discharge line capacity from the table as 6.6tons. This assumes a 100ft length which is consistent with the problem statement. Read note #5 below the table and recognize the condensing temperature is assumed to be 0◦F. However, there is a small table of correction factors for other condensing temperatures. For the condensing temperature given, 10◦F, it is necessary to multiply the table capacity by a factor of 1.2. DischargeCapacity =(6.6tons)(1.2)=7.92tons Answer D 345

problems.statement: Machine A produces a sound pressure of 58dB, and machine B pro- duces a sound pressure of 52dB. What is the combined sound pres- sure?
problems.solution: duces a sound pressure of 52dB. What is the combined sound pres- sure? A. 53dB B. 58dB C. 59dB D. 61dB RefertothetableforCombiningTwoSoundLevels. Notethatwhenthedi(cid:27)erencebetweenthedB levels of the two sources is between 5 and 9, the number of dB to be added to the highest source is 1dB. Since Machine A is the higher source, add 1dB to its sound pressure level. 58dB+1dB =59dB Answer C

problems.statement: The background noise in an offce has a sound pressure level of 38dB. An overhead fan coil unit with a sound pressure level of 43dB turns on. What is the combined sound pressure level?
problems.solution: 38dB. An overhead fan coil unit with a sound pressure level of 43dB turns on. What is the combined sound pressure level? A. 39dB B. 44dB C. 46dB D. 52dB Refer to the table for Combining Two Sound Levels. Note that when the di(cid:27)erence between the dB levels of two sources is between 5 and 9, the number of dB to be added to the highest source is 1dB. Combine the two sources. 43dB+1dB =44dB Answer B 346

problems.statement: Four identical fans produce a combined sound pressure level of 90dBA as measured at a point that is equidistant from each fan. What is the sound pressure level if three fans are shut down? 72
problems.solution: 90dBA as measured at a point that is equidistant from each fan. What is the sound pressure level if three fans are shut down? A. 70dB B. 80dB C. 84dB D. 87dB Refer to the table for Combining Two Sound Levels and note that when the di(cid:27)erence between two source’s sound levels is 0dB, the number of decibels to be added to the highest level is 3dB. In this situation, sources are being removed as fans are shut down, but the same principle applies in reverse. To be clear, the table does not o(cid:27)er a way to add 3 or more sources together directly, therefore it is necessary to (cid:28)nd an approach that allows adding the sources in pairs only. First, imagine shutting 2 of the 4 fans down. One pair of fans may be treated as one source and the other pair treated as a second source. The sound pressure level will be reduced by 3dB when have the sources are removed. This reasoning can be con(cid:28)rmed by imagining re-enabling the 2 fans, thereby combining two pairs of sources, and adding back the 3dB. 90dB−3dB =87dB Next imagine shutting down one of the two remaining fans, thereby removing half the sources. By the same reasoning, another 3dB reduction will be observed. 87dB−3dB =84dB Again, sense check this answer by adding two 84dB sources together to get 87dB, then adding the two pair of sources to get back to 90dB. Answer C

problems.statement: The background noise in a factory prior to any equipment turning on has a sound pressure level of 40dB. Once the equipment is oper- ating, the sound pressure level is 46dB. What is the sound pressure level attributable to the machinery only?
problems.solution: on has a sound pressure level of 40dB. Once the equipment is oper- ating, the sound pressure level is 46dB. What is the sound pressure level attributable to the machinery only? A. 6dB B. 41dB C. 43dB D. 45dB RefertothetableforCombiningTwoSoundLevels. Theprocessforcombiningsoundlevelsinvolves addingupto3dB tothehighest source,dependingonthedi(cid:27)erenceinsoundlevelbetweenthetwo sources. In this case, it is not immediately clear whether the background noise or the machinery is theloudersource. Sincethemostthatcouldbeaddedtoarriveatthecombinedsoundpressurelevel 347 is 3dB, it can be inferred that the background noise is not as loud at the machinery, as combining two 40dB sources would result in a combined level of only 43dB. Therefore, the machinery must be louder. Test values for the machinery between 41dB and 45dB. SPL =41dB →Difference=1dB →CombinedSPL=41dB+3dB =44dB (cid:54)=46dB machinery SPL =42dB →Difference=2dB →CombinedSPL=42dB+2dB =44dB (cid:54)=46dB machinery SPL =43dB →Difference=3dB →CombinedSPL=43dB+2dB =45dB (cid:54)=46dB machinery SPL =44dB →Difference=4dB →CombinedSPL=44dB+2dB =46dB =46dB machinery SPL =45dB →Difference=5dB →CombinedSPL=45dB+1dB =46dB =46dB machinery Note there are two viable answer choices, 44dB and 45dB. However, only 45dB is an answer choice. Answer D

problems.statement: The handle of a large manual valve is secured with a square bolt pattern, as shown. The handle is 18in long and the force applied to the end of the handle during valve opration is 30lb . The bolts have f a nominal diameter of 1/ 4 in and a cross-sectional area of 0.03in2. When the valve is being closed, which statement can be made about the shear stress experienced by the bolts?

choices.A: Bolts A and B experience higher shear stress than bolts C and D.
choices.B: Bolts C and D experience higher shear stress than bolts A and B.
choices.C: Bolts A and C experience higher shear stress than bolts B and D.
choices.D: Bolts B and D experience higher shear stress than bolts A and C.

problems.solution: pattern, as shown. The handle is 18in long and the force applied to the end of the handle during valve opration is 30lb . The bolts have f a nominal diameter of 1/ 4 in and a cross-sectional area of 0.03in2. When the valve is being closed, which statement can be made about the shear stress experienced by the bolts? A. Bolts A and B experience higher shear stress than bolts C and D. B. Bolts C and D experience higher shear stress than bolts A and B. C. Bolts A and C experience higher shear stress than bolts B and D. D. Bolts B and D experience higher shear stress than bolts A and C. This is a qualitative problem, so there is no need to quantify the loads applied to the bolts. Since allboltshavethesamediameterandcross-sectionalarea,themaximumshearstresswillbeadirect outcome of the resultant force experienced due to the applied load and moment. Since the applied load is downward, the reaction forces on the bolts from the applied load are all upward. Since the load is applied a distance away from the bolts, it produces a clockwise moment, generating reaction forces perpendicular to lines drawn through each bolt and the center of the bolt pattern. In othwer words, the forces resulting from the moment are tangent to a circle drawn through all four bolts. Adding the vectors for the reaction forces from the applied load and the moment, notice the directions of the reaction forces partially o(cid:27)set for bolts A and B. However, the forces are partially additiveforboltsCandD.Therefore,boltsCandDhavelargerresultantreactionforcesandmore shear stress than bolts A and B. Answer B 354

problems.statement: The displacement of a hydraulic press is 3in3 per revolution and the pump speed is 1800rpm. The hydraulic pressure is 600psig. The positive displacement pump is 80% effcient and is driven by a 3- phase, 208V AC motor that is 95% effcient. The power factor is 0.9. What size circuit breaker should be selected to protect the system?
problems.solution: the pump speed is 1800rpm. The hydraulic pressure is 600psig. The positive displacement pump is 80% e(cid:30)cient and is driven by a 3- phase, 208V AC motor that is 95% e(cid:30)cient. The power factor is 0.9. What size circuit breaker should be selected to protect the system? A. 20A B. 30A C. 40A D. 50A By default, it is common practice to apply the formulas for hydraulic horsepower based on rules of thumb. However, fundamentally the power produced by a pump is the product of the pressure added by the pump, ∆P, and the volume (cid:29)ow rate, Q. The other details are unit conversions and e(cid:30)ciencies. It is possible and occassionally necessary to build up the formulation from this fundamental concept, as with this problem. Suppose hydraulic horsepower may be expressed simply as below, provided we address units and e(cid:30)ciencies later on. WHP =∆P ×Q In this case, the hydraulic pressure is generated entirely by the pump and ∆P may be taken as 600psi. The volume (cid:29)ow rate is not given, however the displacement of the hydraulic press, i.e. volume, is given as well as the rotational speed. This can be developed into volume per unit time, i.e. volume (cid:29)ow rate. Determine the hydraulic horsepower delivered. Convert units to KW for convenience in the subsequent step.
