problems.statement: A company is considering whether to invest in a piece of equipment that will have an initial cost of $100,000 and will produce $50,000 per year in revenue for the next 3 years before maintenance costs and taxes. Maintenence will cost $5000 per year. The tax rate is 30% and straight line depreciation is used for the equipment. The equipment has a salvage value of $20,000 to be realized at the end of year 3. What is the present value of the initiaitve using a 10% rate of return? 76
problems.solution: that will have an initial cost of $100,000 and will produce $50,000 per year in revenue for the next 3 years before maintenance costs and taxes. Maintenence will cost $5000 per year. The tax rate is 30% and straight line depreciation is used for the equipment. The equipment has a salvage value of $20,000 to be realized at the end of year 3. What is the present value of the initiaitve using a 10% rate of return? A. -$62K B. $18K C. $46K D. $211K Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram and make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. Year 0: -$100K Years 1 & 2: Revenue = 50K and Cost = 5K. The pro(cid:28)t before tax is 45K. The taxes may be addressed separately. Year 3: The cash (cid:29)ow is the same as for years 1 & 2 except for an additional $20K in salvage value which will be included a separate one-o(cid:27) future payment. For tax purposes, the pro(cid:28)t which is considered taxable is reduced by the depreciation for that year even though the cash was actually spent in year 0. For straight line depreciation, the depreciated amount for years 1, 2, and 3 is the same. $100,000 D = =$33,333peryear 3years Determine the pro(cid:28)t for tax purposes. Profit=Revenue−(Expenses+Depreciation) Profit=$50,000−($5,000+$33,333)=$11,667 Apply the tax rate to determine the tax liability. Tax=($11,667)(0.3)=$3,500 Subtract the tax from the before-tax pro(cid:28)t (excluding depreciation) to determine the after-tax pro(cid:28)t. This is the net cash (cid:29)ow for years 1 & 2. AftertaxProfit=$45,000−$3,500=$41,500 For year 3, the salvage value, which is not subject to taxes, will be handled as a separate future payment in our present value calculation. In other words, the $41,500 will be considered part of an annualized cash (cid:29)ow with a 3 year period. 358 Calculate the present value. Use the Economic Factor Tables to look up the cash (cid:29)ow factors for P/A and P/F based on an interest rate of 10%. PV =−$100,000+($41,500)(P/A,10%,3)+($20,000)(P/F,10%,3) PV =−$100,000+($41,500)(2.4869)+($2

problems.statement: A company purchases a factory for $1M. The factory will generate a gross annual revenue of $140K and incur annual expenses of $60K. The facility has an expected life of 20 years and no salvage value. Assuming straight-line depreciation and a 30% e(cid:27)ective tax rate, what is the after tax rate of return if the company sells the factory for $800K and exits the investment after 5 years? Ignore tax implications of capital losses at the time of sale.
problems.solution: a gross annual revenue of $140K and incur annual expenses of $60K. The facility has an expected life of 20 years and no salvage value. Assuming straight-line depreciation and a 30% e(cid:27)ective tax rate, what is the after tax rate of return if the company sells the factory for $800K and exits the investment after 5 years? Ignore tax implications of capital losses at the time of sale. A. 1.5% B. 3.5% C. 5.5% D. 7.5% Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. The cash (cid:29)ow in year 0 is -$1M. The cash (cid:29)ow in year 5 is +$800K in addition to the net pro(cid:28)t after tax. To determine the net pro(cid:28)t after tax for years 1 through 5, start by subtracting the expenses from the revenue. ProfitBeforeTax=Revenue−Expenses=$140K−$60K =$80K Becausethereisdepreciation,thetaxesarenotassessedagainstthe$80K.Thetaxableamounnt is reduced by the depreciation. Essentially, depreciation is applied as an expense even though the cash left the business in year 0. For straight line deprecation of $1M of initial cost spread over 20 years: $1,000,000 D = =$50K 20 The taxable income is therefore: TaxableIncome=$80K−$50K =$30K The tax liability based on a 30% tax rate is: Tax=(0.3)($30K)=$9K Determine the Net Pro(cid:28)t After Tax: NetProfitAfterTax=ProfitBeforeTax−Tax=$80K−$9K =$71K Write an expression for the present value of the investment accounting for the initial cost, the net pro(cid:28)t after tax for years 1 through 5, and the salvage value in year 5. Set the present value equal to zero. By de(cid:28)nition, the interest rate that gives the investment a present value of zero is the rate of return. PV =−$1,000,000+$71,000(P/A,i,5)+$800,000(P/F,i,5)=0 360 The math required for a direct solution becomes complex, so it is faster and equally reliable to use trial an error and interpolation to determine the interest rate. Guesses are best made in the vicinity of the answer choices with a strong preference for values o(cid:27)ered in the Economic

problems.statement: A project has an initial cost of $200K and generates $30K per year in net profits. After 15 years there is a salvage value of $40K. What is the return on investment for the project?
problems.solution: year in net pro(cid:28)ts. After 15 years there is a salvage value of $40K. What is the return on investment for the project? A. 13.3% B. 14.2% C. 15.1% D. 16.0% Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. For year 0 there is an initial cost of -$200K. For years 1 through 15 there is an annual pro(cid:28)t of $30K. For year 15 there is a salvage value of $40K. Write an expressions for the present value of the project. Set the present value equal to zero. Thereturnoninvestment(ROI)istheinterestratethatgivestheinitiativeapresentvalueofzero. PV =−$200,000+$30,000(P/A,i%,15)+$40,000(P/F,i%,15)=0 Themathinvolvedinsolvingfortheinterestrateischallengingandtimeconsuming,thereforea trialanderrormethodisoftenthebestwayforwardfromthispoint. Onewaytogetanapproximate answer to look at the answer choices and notice they are all between 12% and 18%. Another way istonoticethattheonetimesalvagevalueismuchlesssigni(cid:28)cant(thoughnotnegligible)thanthe annual pro(cid:28)t, so it can be temporarily ignored as a (cid:28)rst pass. PV =−$200,000+$30,000(P/A,i%,15)≈0 $200K (P/A,i%,15)≈ ≈6.7 $30K Refering to the i = 12% Factor Table, the value for (P/A,i%,15) is 6.8109 which is slightly higher than 6.7. Since P/A gets smaller as interest rates increase, the ROI must be greater than 12%. Also, the salvage value (previously ignored) is positive and drives the present value up which meansfuturecash(cid:29)owswillneedtobediscountedslightlymoreandtheinterestrateisgoingtobe greater than 12%. This is about as far as qualitative judgment goes. To arrive at a precice answer, it is necessary to (cid:28)nd the PV using interest rates of 12% and 18%, then interpolate. PV =−$200,000+$30,000(P/A,12%,15)+$40,000(P/F,12%,15) i=12 PV =−$200,000+$30,000(6.8109)+$40,000(0.1827)=$11,635>0 i=12 Therefore, ROI >12% PV =−$200,000+$30,000(P/A,18%,15)+$40,000(P/F,18%,15) i=18 PV =−$200,000+$30,000(5.0916)+$40,000(0.0835)=−$43,912<0 i=18 362 Therefore, ROI <18% Interpolate and solve for i.

problems.statement: A $100,000 investment today pays back $10,000 in years 1 through 9 and $100,000 in year 10. What is the rate of return? 77
problems.solution: 9 and $100,000 in year 10. What is the rate of return? A. 7.8% B. 8.6% C. 9.4% D. 10.3% The rate of return is the interest rate that makes the present value zero. Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. There is an initial outlay of $100,000 in year zero (negative). In years 1-9 there is a return of $10,000 per year. In year 10, there is a return of the original $100,000 principle. Write an expressions for the present value and set it equal to zero. PV =−$100,000+$10,000(P/A,i,9)+$100,000(P/F,i,10)=0 Direct solutions are challenging when the interest rate is unknown. The fastest approach is to guess values for i in close proximity to the answer choices. It is also possible to try the answer choices by trial and error; however, since only certain integer percentages can be found in the Economic Factor Tables, this would require using the Economic Factor Conversions to derive the cash (cid:29)ow factors. This solution will describe the process without testing answer choices. Assume i=8%. PV =−$100,000+$10,000(6.2469)+$100,000(0.4632)=$8,789>0 Sincethepresentvalueisgreater thanzero,theinterestratemustbehigher,therebydiscounting the future (positive) cash (cid:29)ows more and reducing the present value. Assume i=10%. 363 PV =−$100,000+$10,000(5.7590)+$100,000(0.3855)=−$3,860<0 Since the present value is less than zero, the interest rate must be lower, thereby discounting the future (positive) cash (cid:29)ows less and increasing the present value. Looking at the answer choices, it is possible at this stage to eliminate choices A and D as they are outside the range of 8-10% which is now known to contain the interest rate that drives the present to zero. Furthermore, it is possible to infer that the interest rate will be closer to 10% than 8% since the resulting present value obtained for i=10% was closer to zero. If time allows, interpolate and solve for i. i[%] PV [$] 8 8,789 i 0 10 −3,860 i−8 0−8,789 = =0.695 10−8 −3,860−8,789 i−8=1.39 i=9.4%

problems.statement: A contractor leases a piece of equipment for $50K down and $30K per year for a project expected to take 3 years. After the third year, the equipment is still needed due to a schedule delay and the contractor must pay $40K per year to continue to lease for the fourth and fifth years. After the fifth year, the project is com- pleted and the equipment is returned. How much annual revenue is required to ensure the project has a 18% return on investment?
problems.solution: per year for a project expected to take 3 years. After the third year, the equipment is still needed due to a schedule delay and the contractor must pay $40K per year to continue to lease for the fourth and (cid:28)fth years. After the (cid:28)fth year, the project is com- pleted and the equipment is returned. How much annual revenue is required to ensure the project has a 18% return on investment? A. $33K B. $44K C. $49K D. $56K Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. For year 0 there is a payment (negative cash (cid:29)ow) of -$50K. For years 1 through 3 there is a negative cash (cid:29)ow of -$30K. For years 4 and 5 there is a negative cash (cid:29)ow of -$40K. For years 1 through 5 there is a positive cash (cid:29)ow of R, the unknown revenue. Writeanexpressionforthepresentvalue. Forconvenience,overstatethecostsinyears1through 3 by showing a negative -$40K cash (cid:29)ow for the entire 5 years, then o(cid:27)set with a positive cash (cid:29)ow 364 of $10K for the (cid:28)rst 3 years only. (Annualized cash (cid:29)ows can only be used if they start in year 1. Otherwise years 4 and 5 would have to be dealt with as independent future payments of an additional $10K which is equally valid but creates a bit more work.) Set the present value equal to zero and determine the revenue that will make the ROI 18%. Use the 18% Factor Table as needed. PV =−$50,000−$40,000(P/A,18%,5)+$10,000(P/A,18%,3)+R(P/A,18%,5)=0 PV =−$50,000−$40,000(3.1272)+$10,000(2.1743)+R(3.1272)=0 −$50,000−$125,088+$21,743+R(3.1272)=0 R(3.1272)=$153,345 R=$49,036 Answer B

problems.statement: Acompanyundertakesanenergysavinginitiativethatcosts$100,000 up front and $1000 per month for recurring service. The project will save $30,000 per year. The equipment involved in the upgrade will have a salvage value of $40,000 after 12 years. What is the annual savings for the project if the interest rate is 7%?
problems.solution: up front and $1000 per month for recurring service. The project will save $30,000 per year. The equipment involved in the upgrade will have a salvage value of $40,000 after 12 years. What is the annual savings for the project if the interest rate is 7%? A. $3,200 B. $7,600 C. $18,600 D. $32,800 Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. Since the problem is asking for annual savings, this solution treats costs as negative and revenues as positive. For year 0 there is a payment for the original purchase of -$100K. For years 1 through 12 there is an annualized cost of $1K per month which equals $12K per year and a savings of $30K per year for a net annual savings of $18K per year. For year 12 there is a positive cash (cid:29)ow of $40K for the salvage value. Write an expression for the annualized savings. Only the initial cost and salvage value need to be transformed to annualized (cid:28)gures. EUAC =$18,000−$100,000(A/P,7%,12)+$40,000(A/F,7%,12) Since there is no Factor Table for 7%, there are two workarounds for calulating the cash (cid:29)ow factors needed. The (cid:28)rst is to use the 6% and 8% tables and interpolate i.e. take the average to get the 7% cash (cid:29)ow factors. 365 N =12 A/P A/F 6% 0.1193 .0593 7% 0.1260 0.0560 8% 0.1327 0.0527 The alternative is to use the Economic Factor Conversions table to (cid:28)nd A/F and A/P. i(1+i)n 0.07(1.07)12 (A/P,7%,12)= = =0.1259 (1+i)n−1 (1.07)12−1 i 0.07 (A/F,7%,12)= = =0.0559 (1+i)n−1 (1.07)12−1 Determine the annual savings. EUAC =$18,000−$100,000(0.1259)+$40,000(0.0559)=$7646 Answer B

problems.statement: A company invests $100K to make a product which will generate $25K of annual revenue over the next 12 years. Expenses will be $5,000 per year. The manufactured product is depreciated over the 12 years using straight-line depreciation. There is no salvage value as all the product is expected to be sold. The income tax rate is 35%. problems.solution: $25K of annual revenue over the next 12 years. Expenses will be $5,000 per year. The manufactured product is depreciated over the 12 years using straight-line depreciation. There is no salvage value as all the product is expected to be sold. The income tax rate is 35%. What is the after-tax rate of return? A. −0.5% B. 5.9% C. 11.7% D. 18.3% Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. In Year 0, there is an initial payment of $100K (negative). InYears1-12, thereisanetpro(cid:28)tbeforetaxof$25K−$5K =$20K. Tocalculatethetax, itis necessary to consider the depreciation. Depreciation is not an actual cash (cid:29)ow; rather, it is applied as a deduction from the pro(cid:28)ts, thereby lowering the taxable income for that year. Depreciation is like a (cid:28)ctitious expense which provides tax bene(cid:28)ts of o(cid:27)setting income without actually spending in that year. Straight line deperciation is calculated using the formula below, where C is the initial cost, S is the salvage value (if applicable), and n is the number of years. n C−S $100,000−$0 D = n = =$8333 j n 12 Determine the tax basis (i.e. taxable income). Taxbasis=$20,000−$8333=$11,667 366 Apply the tax rate to calculate the tax. Tax=(0.35)($11,667)=$4083 Since tax is an acutal expense, subtract the tax from the net pro(cid:28)t before tax to obtain the net pro(cid:28)t after tax. This (cid:28)gure summarizes the annualized cash (cid:29)ows for years 1 through 12. Netprofitaftertax=$20,000−$4083=$15,917 Write an expression for the present value. The after-tax rate of return is the interest rate that makes the present value equal zero. Solve for cash (cid:29)ow factor (P/A,i,12). PV =−$100,000+$15,917(P/A,i,12)=0 (P/A,i,12)=6.2827 Review the Economic Factor Tables and note that the cash (cid:29)ow factor P/A with a duration of 12 years decreases as the interest rate increases, because future cash (cid:29)ows are discounted more as the interest rate increases. problems.statement: A new piece of equipment costs $100,000 and increases revenue by $15,000 per year for the next 6 years, and by $20,000 per year (from the original baseline) for the following 6 years. Assuming no maintenance costs and no salvage value, what is the present value of investing in this equipment if the e(cid:27)ective annual interest rate is 6%?
problems.solution: by $15,000 per year for the next 6 years, and by $20,000 per year (from the original baseline) for the following 6 years. Assuming no maintenance costs and no salvage value, what is the present value of investing in this equipment if the e(cid:27)ective annual interest rate is 6%? A. $43,000 B. $56,000 367 C. $145,000 D. $195,000 Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. In year zero, there is an initial cost of $100,000 (negative). In years 1 through 6, there is an annual revenue of $15,000. In years 7-12, there is an annual revenue of $20,000. Rather than handle the (6) additional $5,000 cash (cid:29)ows in years 7-12 as individual future cash (cid:29)ows, it is faster to assume and annual revenue of $20,000 for the entire 12 years, then compensate foroverstatingtherevenueinyears1-6bysubtracting $5,000peryearover6yearsfromtheoutset. This can be handled as an annualized cash (cid:29)ow over 6 years. The present value can be determined with the following expression. PV =−$100,000+($20,000)(P/A,6%,12)−($5,000)(P/A,6%,6) Usethe6%FactorTabletolookupthecash(cid:29)owfactorsneededtotranslatethecash(cid:29)owsinto present value. Solve for the present value. PV =−$100,000+($20,000)(8.3838)−($5,000)(4.9173)=$43,090 Answer A

problems.statement: A fan supplies air through a 12in by 18in rectangular sheet metal duct with 1in fiberglass insulation. The duct run is 15ft long. Local octave band measurements of the sound power level for the fan are 125Hz, 95dB; 250Hz, 94dB; 500Hz, 92dB; 1000Hz, 90dB; 2000Hz, 85dB; 4000Hz, 69dB. What is the expected sound power level for the 1000Hz octave band at the end of the duct run?
problems.solution: duct with 1in (cid:28)berglass insulation. The duct run is 15ft long. Local octave band measurements of the sound power level for the fan are 125Hz, 95dB; 250Hz, 94dB; 500Hz, 92dB; 1000Hz, 90dB; 2000Hz, 85dB; 4000Hz, 69dB. What is the expected sound power level for the 1000Hz octave band at the end of the duct run? A. 35dB B. 52dB C. 69dB D. 86dB Refer to the table Insertion Loss for Rectangular Sheet Metal Ducts with 1 in. Fiberglass Lining. Look up dimensions 12in by 18in and note the insertion loss for the 1000Hz octave band is 3.7dB. ft Multiply the loss per foot times the length of the duct to obtain the total dB reduction. (cid:18) (cid:19) dB 3.7 (15ft)=55.5dB ft Subtract the dB reduction from the measured sound power level for the 1000Hz octave band to obtain the (cid:28)nal sound power level with the indulated duct inserted. 90dB−55.5dB =34.5dB Answer A

problems.statement: A normal shock wave in air has a Mach number of 3. The pressure upstream is 1atm, what is the pressure downsteam?
problems.solution: upstream is 1atm, what is the pressure downsteam? A. 2 psi B. 10 psi C. 60 psi D. 150 psi Therearetwopossibleapproachesforthisproblem, the(cid:28)rstusingtheNormalShockRelationships table and the second using equations relating downstream (cid:29)ow conditions to upstream (cid:29)ow con- ditions for a normal shock wave. In both cases, the subscript 1 is used to represent the upstream conditions and the subscript 2 is used to represent the downstream conditions. Using the table, for M =3, (cid:28)nd the corresponding pressure ratio. 1 P 2 =10.3333 P 1 375 Since the upstream pressure is known, substitute for P and convert from atm to psi. 1 (cid:18) (cid:19) 14.7psi P =10.3333(P )=10.3333(1atm) =152psi 2 1 atm Alternatively, since the table values have been generated for convenience, it is also valid to use the underlying formulas to solve for the pressure ratio. The ratio of speci(cid:28)c heats, k, is assumed to be 1.4. (cid:18) (cid:19) P 2 = 1 (cid:2) 2kM2−(k−1) (cid:3) P k+1 1 1 (cid:18) (cid:19) P 2 = 1 (cid:2) 2(1.4)(3)2−(1.4−1) (cid:3) =10.3333 P 1.4+1 1 The rest of the solution follows from the (cid:28)rst approach. Answer D

problems.statement: A company purchases a factory for $1M with a salvage value of $300Kin15years. Operationsandmaintenancecostsare$40K/year. At an interest rate of 8%, what is the equivalent uniform annual cost of the factory over the next 15 years? 81
problems.solution: $300Kin15years. Operationsandmaintenancecostsare$40K/year. At an interest rate of 8%, what is the equivalent uniform annual cost of the factory over the next 15 years? A. $70K B. $90K C. $150K D. $170K Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. Since the problem is asking for EUAC, this solution treats costs as positive. For year 0 there is a payment for the original purchase of $1M. For years 1 through 15 there is an annualized payment for operation and maintenance of $40K per year. For year 15 there is a positive cash (cid:29)ow of $300K for the salvage value which partially o(cid:27)sets the costs. Since the O&M cost in years 1 through 15 is already annualized, there is no need for further manipulation. The initial cost and the salvage value need to be transformed into annualized cash (cid:29)ows and added to the O&M. Use the Factor Table for 8% to look up the required cash (cid:29)ow factors. Solve for the EUAC. EUAC =$40,000+$1,000,000(A/P,8%,15)−$300,000(A/F,8%,15) EUAC =$40,000+$116,800−11,040=$145,760 Answer C 376

problems.statement: A commercial tenant entering into a 17 year lease has the option to make a down payment to lower the monthly rent. Assuming a 6% interest rate, which option has the best present value?
problems.solution: to make a down payment to lower the monthly rent. Assuming a 6% interest rate, which option has the best present value? A. $0 down, $12,500/month B. $250K down, $10,000/month C. $500K down, $8,000/month D. $1M down, $5,000/month Find the present value for each option. The the i = 6% Factor Table to (cid:28)nd P/A. The minimum present value is the best choice. PV =(12)($12,500)(P/A,6%,17) 1 PV =(12)($12,500)(10.4773)=$1,571,595 1 PV =$250,000+(12)($10,000)(P/A,6%,17) 2 PV =$250,000+(12)($10,000)(10.4773)=$1,507,276 2 PV =$500,000+(12)($8,000)(P/A,6%,17) 3 PV =$500,000+(12)($8,000)(10.4773)=$1,505,821 3 PV =$1,000,000+(12)($5,000)(P/A,6%,17) 4 PV =$1,000,000+(12)($5,000)(10.4773)=$1,628,638 4 Answer C 377

problems.statement: A group of machines are maintained under a contract which costs $50,000 this year. The contract cost will inrease by $1000 per year over the next 10 years. What is the present value of the entire 10 years of maintenance using an e(cid:27)ctive annual interest rate of 12%.
problems.solution: $50,000 this year. The contract cost will inrease by $1000 per year over the next 10 years. What is the present value of the entire 10 years of maintenance using an e(cid:27)ctive annual interest rate of 12%. A. $303,000 B. $336,000 C. $352,000 D. $370,000 The present value can be represented as the sum of two cash (cid:29)ows: a recurring annual cost of $50,000 and a uniform gradient of $1,000. The gradient has a value of zero in the (cid:28)rst year, such that the (cid:28)rst year cost is $50,000, second year is $51,000, third year is $52,000, etc. There is no initialcostre(cid:29)ectedinyear0asitiscustomaryinengineeringeconomicstore(cid:29)ectcoststhatoccur throughout the year at the end of the year. Write an expression for the present value. PV =A(P/A,12%,10)+G(P/G,12%,10) Use the 12% Factor Table to look up the cash (cid:29)ow factors needed to translate the cash (cid:29)ows into present value. Solve for the present value. PV =($50,000)(5.6502)+($1,000)(20.2541)=$302,764 Answer A

A. $2670 B. $3490 C. $3750 D. $5030 The cost is a function of electrical power and time, and electrical power is a function of hydraulic horsepower (aka water horsepower i.e. whp) and e(cid:30)ciency. Start by calculating the water horse- power based on volume (cid:29)ow rate and feet of head provided by the pump: Q∆h (200)(130) whp= = =6.566hp 3960 3960 378 Note the volume and head units must be in GPM and ft, respectively, to use this (cid:16)rule of thumb(cid:17) equation. Therefore units need not be shown, provided they are con(cid:28)rmed to be correct prior to use. Recall that brake horsepower, bhp, depends on water horsepower, whp, and the e(cid:30)ciency of the pump, η . Similarly, the electrical power, W˙ ,depends on brake horsepower, bhp, and motor p e(cid:30)ciency, η . m whp bhp= η p bhp W˙ = η m Put these together, substitute, solve, and convert to KW: (cid:18) (cid:19) whp 6.566hp .746KW W˙ = = =6.58KW η η (.8)(.93) 1hp p m To (cid:28)nd the annual cost, multiply by time and the unit rate of electricity: (cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19) 12hrs 5days 52wks $0.13 Cost=(6.58KW) =$2669peryear day wk yr KWH Answer A

A. 100cfm B. 300cfm C. 3500cfm D. 8500cfm Since the air being supplied is at an elevated temperature and pressure, it is necessary to make Temperature and Altitude Corrections for Air. This can be achieved using the factors in the table; however,thepressureisgiveninpsiaratherthanasanelevation,thereforeitismorestraightforward to multiply by the ratio of the pressure as compared to standard atmosphereic pressure. Similarly, a temperature ratio multiplier is also convenient to use, provided absolute temperature units are used. Standard temperature and pressure may be considered 60◦F and 14.7psia. Intermsofqualitativeexpectations,ahigherthanstandardpressureimpliesthestandardCFM (SCFM) will be higher than the actual CFM. A higher than standard temperature implies the standard CFM will be lower than the actual CFM. In concept, SCFM is attempt to explain how much volume the compressor would move if pumping air at STP. problems.statement: Methane is burned with 20% excess air. What is the air-to-fuel ratio? lb lb lb lb 85

A. 14lb lb B. 17lb lb C. 21lb lb D. 24lb lb Look up the table Combustion Reactions of Common Fuel Constituents and (cid:28)nd the combustion reaction for methane burned stoichiometrically in air. Include 3.76 nitrogen molecules for every 1 oxygen molecule since nitrogen has been omitted in the table. Nitrogen does not participate in the reaction; however, it does contribute mass to the air and thus impacts the air-to-fuel ratio. CH +2O +2(3.76)N →CO +2H O+2(3.76)N 4 2 2 2 2 2 Add 20% excess air. Air-to-fuel ratio applies only to the reactants, so it is not necessary to balance the reaction and write out the product side. CH +1.2[2O +2(3.76)N ]→products 4 2 2 CH +(2.4)O +(9.02)N →products 4 2 2 Calculate the air-to-fuel ratio by multiplying the number of moles of each constituent times the atomicweightanddividingtheairmassbythefuelmass. UsethePeriodicTabletolookupatomic weights as needed. m n M +n M (2.4)(32)+(9.02)(28) lb air = O2 O2 N2 N2 = =20.6 m n M (1)(16) lb fuel CH4 CH4 Alternatively, intheCombustionReactionstable, noticetheheadingforStoichiometricOxygen and Air Requirements. Increase this value by 20% to account for excess air. lb (cid:18) (cid:19) lb lb (1.2) 17.24 =20.7 lb lb Answer C 389

A. 44KW B. 59KW C. 66KW D. 89KW The motor output, typically expressed in bhp, is the input to the pump. Therefore, if the pump initially consumes 200hp, this represents the motor output. Since the motor e(cid:30)ciency is assumed to be 100%, the motor’s electrical power demand (input) is equal in magnitude to its bhp (output) and di(cid:27)ers only in its units. problems.statement: During initial operating conditions, the cooling coil in a computer room air conditioning unit cools 90◦F entering air to 65◦F. The unit uses 56◦F supply chilled water to drive the coil temperature to an apparatus dew point of 60◦F. The coil is designed for a maximum air side delta T of 25◦F. After an increase in heat load, the same unit is met with 95◦F return air. In order to stay safely within the coil’s temperature limits, operators raise the chilled water supply temperature to 63◦F such that the apparatus dew point becomes 67◦F. What is the expected discharge air temper- ature based on the new operating conditions? 103
problems.solution: room air conditioning unit cools 90◦F entering air to 65◦F. The unit uses 56◦F supply chilled water to drive the coil temperature to an apparatus dew point of 60◦F. The coil is designed for a maximum air side delta T of 25◦F. After an increase in heat load, the same unit is met with 95◦F return air. In order to stay safely within the coil’s temperature limits, operators raise the chilled water supply temperature to 63◦F such that the apparatus dew point becomes 67◦F. What is the expected discharge air temper- ature based on the new operating conditions? A. 68.3◦F B. 70.0◦F C. 71.7◦F D. 72.5◦F Consider the original operating conditions as Case A and the (cid:28)nal operating conditions as Case B. Calculate the coil e(cid:30)ciency for Case A. Coil e(cid:30)ciency is the ratio of the actual ∆Taccross the coil as compared to the maximum possible ∆T which occurs when the discharge air has been cooled to the apparatus dew point (ADP). T −T 90◦F −65◦F η = return,A discharge,A = =0.833 A T −ADP 90◦F −60◦F return,A A Assume the coil e(cid:30)ciency remains constant for the new set of operating conditions. η =η =0.833 B A Use the new return temperature and new ADP for Case B to determine the new discharge temperature, T , after the change. discharge,B T −T 95◦F −T η = return,B discharge,B = discharge,B =0.833 B T −ADP 95◦F −67◦F return,B B T =71.7◦F discharge,B While it is not required for the solution, it is noteworthy that the reduction in ∆T on the air side will require an increase in cfm to satisfy the same cooling demand, and since the problem states that the heat load has been increased, this compounds the need for additional air(cid:29)ow! Answer C 432

problems.statement: An energy recovery device is used to pre-cool 5000cfm of outside air at 95◦F db / 75◦F wb. An equivalent volume of return air enters the device at 76◦F and 50% relative humidity. After passing through the device the exhaust air is discharged outside at 84◦F db / 70◦F wb. What is the reduction in cooling demand provided by the energy recovery wheel?
problems.solution: air at 95◦F db / 75◦F wb. An equivalent volume of return air enters the device at 76◦F and 50% relative humidity. After passing through the device the exhaust air is discharged outside at 84◦F db / 70◦F wb. What is the reduction in cooling demand provided by the energy recovery wheel? A. 4tons B. 8tons C. 10tons D. 18tons Sketch the energy recovery device, considering the outside air as State 1, the supply air as State 2, the exhaust air as State 3, and the return air as State 4. Since the problem statement makes no reference to any losses or e(cid:27)ectiveness, assume 100% of the heat absorbed by the exhaust stream from 4→3 is removed from the supply stream 1→2. For an energy recovery device, both sensible andlatentheataretransferred,soitisappropriatetoquantifythetotalheatgainedbytheexhaust path using the change in enthalpy which accounts for both sensible and latent energy. State 3 and State 4 are both fully de(cid:28)ned, so use the Psychrometric Chart to obtain the enthalpies, h and h . 3 4 433 T =84◦F 3,db T =70◦F 3,wb Btu h =33.96 3 lb T =76◦F 4 φ =50% 4 Btu h =28.74 4 lb Use the total heating rule of thumb to calculate the total heat added to the exhaust stream, and thus removed from the supply stream. Convert to refrigeration tons. Btu Q˙ =4.5cfm∆h=4.5(5000)(33.96−28.74)=117,450 t hr 117,450Btu Q˙ = hr =9.8tons t 12,000 Btu hr·ton Answer C

problems.statement: A 75% e(cid:27)ective enthalpy wheel supplies 2000cfm of tempered air by recovering energy from a return air stream at 74◦F and 50% relative humidity. The entering outside air is 96◦F db / 76◦F wb. What is the dry bulb temperature of the tempered air?
problems.solution: by recovering energy from a return air stream at 74◦F and 50% relative humidity. The entering outside air is 96◦F db / 76◦F wb. What is the dry bulb temperature of the tempered air? A. 76◦F B. 79◦F C. 82◦F D. 85◦F AnenthalpywheelisanexampleofanEnergy-RecoveryVentilator(ERV).Therefore,bothsensible andlatentenergyaretransferredthroughthedevice. However, thequestionisconcernedonlywith the dry bulb temperature of the tempered air, therefore it is not necessary to consider the enthalpy values in this case. (To be clear, it wouldn’t be incorrect or inappropriate to explore the enthalpies as both sensible and latent heat are being conveyed when using an enthalpy wheel; it simply is not necessary in this case.) The e(cid:27)ectiveness of the enthalpy wheel applies to both the sensible and latent heat being transfered in an equal manner. In other words, 75% of the possible sensible heat is being transferred, and 75% of the possible latent heat is being transferred. 434 Sketch the enthalpy wheel and label with all given information. Equate the e(cid:27)ectiveness to the ratiooftheactualdeltaTachievedbetweentheoutsideairandthetemperedairascomparedtothe maximum delta T possible, which would be the di(cid:27)erence between the outside air and the return air. It is recommended to arrive at this relation through reasoning since the HRV/ERV formulas in the Reference Handbook are unecessarily convoluted. T −T ε= OA TA T −T OA RA 96◦F −T 0.75= TA 96◦F −74◦F T =79.5◦F TA Answer B

problems.statement: The overall effciency of a gas turbine combined cycle is 55%. The gas turbine cycle standing alone has an effciency of 40%. What is the effciency of the Rankine cycle?
problems.solution: gas turbine cycle standing alone has an e(cid:30)ciency of 40%. What is the e(cid:30)ciency of the Rankine cycle? A. 9% B. 25% C. 75% D. 91% Refer to the Brayton Cycle for the gas turbine standing alone. Refer to the Combined Cycle when waste heat recovery is included. The heat recovery section can be modeled as a Rankine Cycle. The product of the losses from the Brayton Cycle and the losses from the Rankine Cycle equals the losses from the Combined Cycle. Solve for the e(cid:30)ciency of the Rankine Cycle. (1−η )(1−η )=(1−η ) Brayton Rankine Combined (1−0.4)(1−η )=(1−0.55) Rankine (cid:18) (cid:19) 0.45 1−η = =0.75 Rankine 0.6 η =0.25 Rankine Another way to conceptualize this scenario is to notice that since the standalone Brayton Cycle has 40% e(cid:30)ciency, that implies 60% losses. Reason that to increase the overall e(cid:30)ciency of the combined cycle to from 40% to 55%, 15% of the wasted 60% must be converted into useful work, which is one fourth or 25% of the waste energy from the Brayton Cycle which is heat input to the Rankine Cycle. Answer B 437

problems.statement: A 7.5hp motor is 93% effcient. How much waste heat is generated due to losses? hr hr hr hr

Since motors are rated in bhp, the mechanical output power is always equal to the nominal rating of the motor, in this case 7.5hp. Therefore, the losses do not come out of the 7.5hp, but rather, come out of the input electrical power leaving (precisely) the bhp left to be delivered. problems.statement: A plant room contains a generator with a noise level of 95dBA, an air compressor with a noise level of 88dBA, and a pump with a noise level of 77dBA. What is the overall noise level?
problems.solution: air compressor with a noise level of 88dBA, and a pump with a noise level of 77dBA. What is the overall noise level? A. 87dB B. 95dB C. 96dB D. 98dB Refer to the table for Combining Two Sound Levels. Note that when the di(cid:27)erence between the dB levels of two sources is between 5 and 9, the number of dB to be added to the highest source is 1dB. Also recall that only two sources may be added at once; the table provides no guidance on adding more than two sources. Start by combining the two loudest sources. 95dB+1dB =96dB Regarding the pump noise, recognize that the di(cid:27)erence between the noise levels between the pump and combined generator and compressor fall into the ’10dB or more’ range, therefore, there is no meaningful di(cid:27)erence to the combined level. Answer C

problems.statement: A data center developer has a choice between (1) building a $50M data center that will have a 30 year lifespan and cost $1M per year to maintain, and (2) building a $15M data center that will have a 10 year lifespan and cost $3M per year to maintain. If the interest rate is 8%, which alternative is superior?
problems.solution: data center that will have a 30 year lifespan and cost $1M per year to maintain, and (2) building a $15M data center that will have a 10 year lifespan and cost $3M per year to maintain. If the interest rate is 8%, which alternative is superior? A. Option 1 B. Option 2 C. Neither, the options are comparable. D. There is not enough information. Inengineeringeconomics, whenevaluatingalternatives, therearetwobasicapproaches: comparing the present value, and comparing the equivalent uniform annual cost (EUAC). When the two options have the same life cycle, present value is a viable option. However, when the two options have di(cid:27)erent life cycles as is the case here, EUAC is the preferred approach. The maintenance costs are given as annual costs already, so the main task is annualizing the up front cost. Write expressions for the EUAC for both options. Use the i=8% Factor Table to retreive the cash (cid:29)ow factors. 449 EUAC =$50M(A/P,8%,30)+$1M =$50M(0.0888)+$1M =$5.44M 1 EUAC =$15M(A/P,8%,10)+$3M =$15M(0.1490)+$3M =$5.23M 2 Option 2 is superior. Answer B

problems.statement: A company with a tax rate of 30% makes a $50,000 one time pur- chase that drives $8,000 of annual revenue and carries $1200 of an- nual maintenance costs. The salvage value after 10 years is $5000. What is the present value of the investment over 10 years at a 6% interest rate? 108
problems.solution: chase that drives $8,000 of annual revenue and carries $1200 of an- nual maintenance costs. The salvage value after 10 years is $5000. What is the present value of the investment over 10 years at a 6% interest rate? A. −$12,200 B. −$6,000 C. $11,700 D. $52,800 Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. In Year 0, there is an initial payment of $50K (negative). In Years 1-10, there is a net pro(cid:28)t before tax of $8000−$1200 = $6800 and a net pro(cid:28)t after tax of $6800(1−0.3)=$4760. InYear10,thereisalsoafuturepaymentforthesalvagevalueof$5Kinadditiontotheafter-tax pro(cid:28)t. Note the tax rate is applied only to the annual pro(cid:28)ts and not to the initial cost or salvage value, and there is no reference to depreciation in the problem statement. Writeanexpressionforthepresentvalue. Usethei=6%FactorTabletoretreivethecash(cid:29)ow factors. PV =−$50,000+$4760(P/A,6%,10)+$5000(P/F,6%,10) PV =−$50,000+$4760(7.3601)+$5000(0.5584)=−$12,174 Answer A 450

problems.statement: A company pays $500K for a warehouse that it plans to hold for 15 years. The warehouse will save the company $5000 per month in shipping costs, boosting profit. Maintenance and taxes cost $10,000 per year. At the end of 15 years, what sale price is needed to realize an 8% rate of return?
problems.solution: years. The warehouse will save the company $5000 per month in shipping costs, boosting pro(cid:28)t. Maintenance and taxes cost $10,000 per year. At the end of 15 years, what sale price is needed to realize an 8% rate of return? A. $23K B. $230K C. $780K D. $1.7M Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. In Year 0, there is an initial payment of $500K (negative). In Years 1-15, there is a monthly savings of $5K which translates to a $60K increase to the annual revenue. There is also $10K in annual costs, included taxes. Therefore, the net pro(cid:28)t after tax is (12)($5K)−$10K =$50K per year. InYear15,thereisasalvagevalueofunknownmagnitudewhichisbeingsoughtinthisproblem. Write an expression for the present value. The rate of return is the interest rate that makes the present value equal to zero, in this case, i=8%. Use the i=8% Factor Table to retreive the cash (cid:29)ow factors. Solve for S, the salvage value. PV =−$500K+$50K(P/A,8%,15)+S(P/F,8%,15)=0 −$500K+$50K(8.5595)+S(0.3152)=0 S =$228,506 Answer B 451

problems.statement: A power plant upgrade project takes two years to implement and has an initial cost of $500,000 plus an additional $250,000 at the end of year 1 and year 2. An additional $100,000 retainage will be paid at the end of the one-year defects & liability period following project completion. The life cycle of the upgrade is expected to be 20 years from completion and the salvage value will be $300,000. At an interest rate of 6%, what is the annualized cost of the venture?
problems.solution: has an initial cost of $500,000 plus an additional $250,000 at the end of year 1 and year 2. An additional $100,000 retainage will be paid at the end of the one-year defects & liability period following project completion. The life cycle of the upgrade is expected to be 20 years from completion and the salvage value will be $300,000. At an interest rate of 6%, what is the annualized cost of the venture? A. $83,000 B. $91,000 C. $99,000 D. $107,000 Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a summary of cash (cid:29)ows. This solution treats costs as positive. Year 0: $500K Year 1: $250K Year 2: $250K Year 3: $100K Year 20: -$300K The cash (cid:29)ows in years 1 through 3 can be expressed as an annual cost of $100K for 3 years plus an annual cost of an additional $150K for the (cid:28)rst 2 years only. Use the 6% Factor Table in the Economic Analysis section to (cid:28)nd the present value: $500,000+$100,000(P/A,6%,3)+$150,000(P/A,6%,2)−$300,000(P/F,6%,20) $500,000+$100,000(2.673)+$150,000(1.8334)−$300,000(.3118)=$948,770 Find the equivalent annualized cost spread over 20 years at 6%: $948,770(A/P,6%,20)=$948,770(.0872)=$82,733 Alternate Approach: Discount each cash (cid:29)ow back to its present value individually: PV =$500,000 0 (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)n (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)1 PV =$250,000 =$250,000 =$235,849 1 1+i 1.06 (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)n (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)2 PV =$250,000 =$250,000 =$222,499 2 1+i 1.06 (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)n (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)3 PV =$100,000 =$250,000 =$83,962 3 1+i 1.06 452 (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)n (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)20 PV =−$300,000 =−$300,000 =−$93,541 20 1+i 1.06 Take the sum to (cid:28)nd the present value: PV +PV +PV +PV +PV 0 1 2 3 20 $500,000+$235,849+$222,499+$83,962−$93,541=$948,769 Use the same approach as in the original solution to (cid:28)nd the annualized cost: $948,769(A/P,6%,20)=$948,770(.0872)=$82,733 Answer A

problems.statement: A fan delivers 10,000cfm of 70◦F air at sea level. How much volume will the same fan deliver when used at 4000ft elevation to distribute 50◦F air?
problems.solution: will the same fan deliver when used at 4000ft elevation to distribute 50◦F air? A. 8,000cfm B. 9,000cfm C. 11,000cfm D. 12,000cfm Qualitatively, itstandstoreasonthatincreasingtheelevationatwhichthefanisusedwillincrease thecapacityofthefanintermsofvolume(cid:29)owratebecauseairislessdensehigherintheatmosphere. However, reducing the temperature of the air makes it more dense, and reduces the capacity of the fan. These factors will compete and must be considered separately. Search for Temperature and Altitude Corrections and use the table to look up the Density Factors for both the temperature and elevation change. The Density Factor for the temperature requires interpolation. Temperature [◦F] Density Factor 0 1.152 50 DF T 70 1 70−50 1−DF = T =0.2857 70−0 1−1.152 1−DF =−0.0434→DF =1.043 T T Look up the Density Factor for the altitude. DF =0.864 A Although it is not explicitly stated in the reference handbook whether the original cfm should be multiplied or divided by the density factors, the previous reasoning provides the expectation that the decreased temperature will tend to reduce the fan capacity and the increased altitude will tend to increase the fan capacity. Therefore, the original cfm should be divided by the density factors. Q 10,000cfm Q = sealevel = =11,097cfm 4000ft DF ·DF (1.043)(0.864) T A Answer C 454

problems.statement: A heat recovery ventilator is used to pre-heat 30◦F, 40% RH outside air with 75◦F, 50% RH exhaust air. The HRV e(cid:27)ectiveness is 60%. What quantity of heat is recovered? lb lb lb lb
problems.solution: air with 75◦F, 50% RH exhaust air. The HRV e(cid:27)ectiveness is 60%. What quantity of heat is recovered? A. 4.3Btu lb B. 6.5Btu lb C. 10.8Btu lb D. 27.1Btu lb Let State 1 refer to the entering outside air condition. Let State 2 refer to the leaving outside air after being heated through the ventilator. Let State 3 refer to the entering return air condition. Ignore the leaving exhaust air as it is not relevant. Recall the distinction between heat recovery and energy recovery. Energy recovery devices transmitlatentenergyinadditiontosensibleheat. Heat-RecoverydevicesdriveexclusivelySensible Energy Recovery and the humidity need not be considered. Therefore, the e(cid:27)ectivenss of a heat recovery ventilator is given by a the ratio of ∆T to ∆T . (Energy recovery e(cid:27)ectiveness actual ideal would depend on changes in enthalpy rather than temperature.) ∆T T −30◦F ε= actual = 2 =0.6 ∆T 75◦F −30◦F ideal T =57◦F 2 The total heat transfer by the heat recovery device is given by the equation below. Sicne there is no mass (cid:29)ow rate or volume rate given and the problem is asking for the quantity of heat rather than the rate of heat transfer, divide both sides by m˙ and solve for q, heat per unit mass. The delta T is the actual increase in temperature experienced by the outside air. Q˙ =m˙c ∆T p Q˙ (cid:18) Btu (cid:19) Btu =q =c ∆T = 0.24 (57◦F −30◦F)=6.48 m˙ p lb·◦F lb Answer B 455

Solve for the number of active coils. 456 N =N +2 t N =N −2=10−2=8 t Solve for the spring constant. (cid:16) (cid:17) k = d4G = (0.15in)4 10×106 i l n bf 2 =79 lb f 8D3N 8(1in)3(8) in Answer C

problems.statement: A carnot heat pump operates during winter when the outisde air temperature is 20◦F and inside temperature is 68◦F. During sum- mer, the unit runs in reverse to provide cooling when the outisde air temperature is 100◦F and inside temperature is 72◦F. What is the coeffcient of performance during summer operation?
problems.solution: temperature is 20◦F and inside temperature is 68◦F. During sum- mer, the unit runs in reverse to provide cooling when the outisde air temperature is 100◦F and inside temperature is 72◦F. What is the coe(cid:30)cient of performance during summer operation? A. 3 B. 4 C. 17 D. 19 Recall that a Carnot heat pump has the maximum theorteical Coe(cid:30)cient of Performance based on the temperatures of the reservoirs heat is being removed from and rejected to. In this case, winter operation may be ignored since the question is asking about the COP for summer operation only. The COP for refrigeration for a carnot cycle is given by: T COP = L c T −T H L Temperature units must be absolute (Rankine). The denominator is a temperature di(cid:27)erential and therefore may be left in Farenheit. 72◦F +460◦ COP = =19 c 100◦F −72◦F Answer D

problems.statement: A refrigeration cycle using R-123 operates between 30psia and 125psia with no subcooling and isenthalpic expansion. What is the quality of the mixture entering the evaporator?
problems.solution: 125psia with no subcooling and isenthalpic expansion. What is the quality of the mixture entering the evaporator? A. 0.20 B. 0.26 C. 0.32 D. 0.38 Look up the R-123 properties table and Pressure Versus Enthalpy Curves for Refrigerant 123. Consider the high pressure side of the refrigeration cycle after the refrigerant passes through the condenser, it is expected to be a saturated liquid at 125psia. Let this be considered State 3. Use eitherthetableorthecharttodeterminetheenthalpyofsaturatedliquid,h . Thetablelendsitself f to slightly higher precision but may take a bit more time. Formal interpolation is not necessary. Beware of the logarithmic scale on the vertical axis if using the P-H chart. Relative humidity is a function of the partial pressure of water vapor in air, p , and the maximum possible partial w pressure for water vapor in air,p , which occurs at fully saturated conditions i.e. 100% relative ws humidity. The saturation pressure of water vapor in air is a function of temperature. Warmer air has a greater capacity for absorbing moisture, and therefore a higher saturation pressure. p φ= w →p =φp p w ws ws UsethePropertiesofSaturatedWaterandSteam(Temperature)tabletoobtainthesaturation pressure of water vapor at 85◦F. 467 T =85◦F p =0.6psia ws Substitute and solve for the partial pressure of water vapor at 50% relative humidity. p =(0.5)(0.6psia)=0.3psia w Answer A

problems.statement: 500,000L of water is discharged from a reservoir and falls 30meters. s How much power is released due to the change in elevation? 117
problems.solution: s How much power is released due to the change in elevation? A. 60MW B. 150MW C. 310MW D. 480MW Power is released when the potential energy stored in the water is converted into kinetic energy as the water descends from high elevation. Search for Potential Energy and use the formula for potential energy due to gravity. PE =mgh Work is a form of energy, and power is work per unit time. On the left side, potential energy, PE, can be replaced with work, W˙ , and on the right side use the mass (cid:29)ow rate, m˙, instead of the mass, m. Both sides then have time in the denominator. W˙ =m˙gh 1L of water has a mass of 1kg. This can be con(cid:28)rmed by searching Properties of Water at Standard Conditions which states that ρ =1000kg. Since 1000L=1m3, then converting the water m3 density to kg. problems.statement: Decane (C H ) undergoes complete, stoichiometric combustion in 10 22 air. What is the mass fraction of carbon dioxide in the product gas?
problems.solution: 10 22 air. What is the mass fraction of carbon dioxide in the product gas? A. 13% B. 19% C. 49% D. 69% Decane is not listed in the table Combustion Reactions of Common Fuel Constituents. Therefore, it is necessary to write the balanced reaction. Since the combustion is stoichiometric, there is no excessair. Startbywritingtheproductsandreactantsusingarbitraryconstantcoe(cid:30)cients. Forthe reactants, there are 3.77 nitrogen molecules per oxygen molecule in air. The products are carbon dioxide, water vapor, and nitrogen. Nitrogen does not participate in the reaction. C H +x(O +3.77N )→aCO +bH O+cN 10 22 2 2 2 2 2 Balance the carbon. a=10 Balance the hydrogen. 22=2b→b=11 Balance the oxygen. 2x=2a+b=2(10)+11=31 31 x= =15.5 2 Balance the nitrogen. 2c=2(3.77)x=2(3.77)(15.5)=116.87 116.87 c= =58.435 2 Re-write the product side of the reaction with all known coe(cid:30)ents. Reactants→10CO +11H O+58.435N 2 2 2 Determine the Mass Fraction of carbon dioxide in the product gas. Use the Periodic Table to look up atomic weights as required. The mass of each constiuent is the product of the number of moles and the molecular weight. m m y = CO2 = CO2 CO2 (cid:80) m m +m +m i CO2 H2O N2 502 (10)[12+2(16)] y = =0.19=19% CO2 (10)[12+2(16)]+11[2(1)+16]+58.435[2(14)] Answer B

problems.statement: A 1500cfm two-pipe fan coil unit with electric reheat maintains a room at 74◦F and 50% relative humidity. The supply air from the unit is 58◦F. The cooling coil discharge condition is 48◦F db / 46◦F wb. There is 1◦F of temperature rise across the fan. Neglecting losses, how much power is required to run the reheat coil? 126
problems.solution: room at 74◦F and 50% relative humidity. The supply air from the unit is 58◦F. The cooling coil discharge condition is 48◦F db / 46◦F wb. There is 1◦F of temperature rise across the fan. Neglecting losses, how much power is required to run the reheat coil? A. 4300W B. 4700W C. 5200W D. 5700W The electric reheat coil provides sensible heating only. Use the sensible heating rule of thumb for air. Q˙ =1.08cfm∆T htg The volume (cid:29)ow rate is given. Air enters the heating coil after leaving the cooling coil at a dry bulb temperature of 48◦F. Since the fan adds 1◦F of temperature rise to achieve the 58◦F supply air temperaure from the unit, the air leaving the reheat coil and entering the fan is 57◦F. Solve for the reheat load, and convert units to W. (cid:32) (cid:33) Btu 1W Q˙ =1.08(1500)(57−48)=14,580 =4273W htg hr 3.412Btu hr Answer A 503

problems.statement: A pump located 10ft above an open water tank at sea level trans- ports 80gpm of 80◦F water. The head loss in the suction piping is 15ft. The net positive suction head required is 4ft. What is the net positive suction head available?
problems.solution: ports 80gpm of 80◦F water. The head loss in the suction piping is 15ft. The net positive suction head required is 4ft. What is the net positive suction head available? A. 8ft B. 18ft C. 28ft D. 38ft Net Positive Suction Head Available is a function of the physical layout of the pump, piping, and source. The Net Positive Suction Head Required is determined from the manufacturer’s speci(cid:28)cations and has no bearing on the NPSHA. The criterion for avoiding cavitation is that NPSH > NPSH . Since this problem only requires calculating NPSHA, the NPSHR is extra A R information. Sketch and label the system. Work through each term in the NPSHA formula. NPSH =h +h −h −h A p z vpa f The (cid:28)rst term, h , is the atmospheric pressure. Use the conversion factor rule of thumb for p water to convert from psi to ft. (cid:18) (cid:19) ft h =(14.7psia) 2.31 ≈34ft p psi The second term, h , is the elevation term and is taken as negative becasue the pump is above z the water source, thereby reducing the head available at the pump inlet. Sometimes the operator before this term is written as (cid:16)plus or minus(cid:17) ± to re(cid:29)ect this arrangement. It is (cid:28)ne to regard the value as positive as long as in the (cid:28)nal step it is subtracted. h =−10ft z The third term, h , is the vapor pressure and is a function of the temperature of the water. vpa The vapor pressure is always subtracted. The higher the water temperature, the higher the vapor pressure, and the harder it will be to avoid cavitation. Use the Properties of Saturated Water and Steam (Temperature) table to look up the saturation pressure at T = 80◦F. Use the conversion factor rule of thumb for water to convert from psi to ft. T =80◦F (cid:18) (cid:19) ft P =0.51psia 2.31 =1.178ft sat psi The fourth term, h , is always subtracted since losses reduce the available head. The value for f h was given. f h =15ft f 504 Calculate the NPSHA. NPSH =34ft−10ft−1.178ft−15ft=7.8ft A Answer A

problems.statement: Refrigerant R-410a is compressed isentropically from a saturated condition at 20psia to a pressure of 190psia. What is the change in temperature during the compression process?
problems.solution: condition at 20psia to a pressure of 190psia. What is the change in temperature during the compression process? A. 11◦F B. 63◦F C. 76◦F D. 174◦F Sketch the compression process on a Pressure-Enthalpy diagram. Use the table for Refrigerant 410A to obtain the saturation temperature corresponding to the compressor inlet pressure, P . 1 Note the table provides two temperatures, ’Bubble’ and ’Dew’. Typically the values are close in magnitude and selection is of little consequence. For simplicity, use the ’Dew’ temperature since this value corresponds to when the last drop of refrigerant evaporates, which should be complete prior to entering the compressor. P =20psia (saturated) 1 T =−49.2◦F 1 Using the chart Pressure Versus Enthalpy Curves for Refrigerant 410A, draw a horizontal line across the chart for the high pressure condition, P = 190psia. To locate the line properly, check 2 the table for the corresponding saturation temperature which is approximately T ≈ 61.6◦F. The 3 goal is to (cid:28)nd T , however, knowing T will help draw the horizontal line contain the condensing 2 3 process from 2 → 3 in the correct location on the chart. The vertical axis uses log scale so using pressure alone can be challenging. It is also essential to recognize that in the superheated region, the temperature is no longer constant along a horizontal line. Next, draw a line of constant entropy starting from State 1 and making best e(cid:27)ort to remain parallel with local constant entropy lines on the chart, which tend to travel north-northeast to south-southwest, but are not perfectly linear. Find the interesection of this constant entropy line from 1 → 2 and the horizontal line containing States 2 & 3. The interesection represents State 2. problems.statement: Propane is burned with approximately 50% excess air as shown in the reaction below. What is the mass fraction of CO in the combustion 2 products? C H +
problems.solution: in the reaction below. What is the mass fraction of CO in the 2 combustion products? C H +7.5O +28.2N →CombustionProducts 3 8 2 2 A. 12% B. 22% C. 32% D. 42% As a starting point, refer to the table on Combustion Reactions of Common Fuel Constituents and note the stoichiometric reaction for propane and air. Note nitrogen is excluded since it does not participate in the reaction; however, it is present in both the reactants and products and should be included since its presense will in(cid:29)uence the mass fraction of any other constituent. The full stoichiometric reaction for propane is: C H +[5O +5(3.76)N ]→3CO +4H O+5(3.76)N 3 8 2 2 2 2 2 Increasetheairby50%onthereactancesideandincludeoxygenontheproductside. Incruduce variables coe(cid:30)cients to the product side of the reaction to allow for balancing. C H +1.5[5O +5(3.76)N ]→aCO +bH O+cO +dN 3 8 2 2 2 2 2 2 C H +7.5O +28.2N →aCO +bH O+cO +dN 3 8 2 2 2 2 2 2 153 Balancing carbon shows that a = 3. Balancing hydrogen shows that b = 4. This is unchanged from the schoichiometric reaction. Balancing nitrogen shows that d = 28.2, which makes sense since nitrogen doesn’t react in the combustion process. Balance oxygen to (cid:28)nd c. Substitute known values for a and b. (2)(7.5)=2a+b+2c 15=6+4+2c c=2.5 The (cid:28)nal balance reaction for propane burned with 50% excess air is: C H +7.5O +28.2N →3CO +4H O+2.5O +28.2N 3 8 2 2 2 2 2 2 To (cid:28)nd the mass fraction of carbon dioxide in the reactants, use the Periodic Table to look up the atomic weights of each element as needed. Divide the mass of CO by the total mass of all 2 reactants. m =3[12+(16)(2)]=132 CO2 m =4[(2)(1)+16]=72 H2O m =2.5[(2)(16)]=80 O2 m =28.2[(2)(14)]=789.6 N2 m 132 CO2 = =0.12 m +m +m +m 132+72+80+789.6 CO2 H2O O2 N2 Answer A 154

In other words, the Mole Fractions would be x = 0.21 and x = 0.79. However, O2 N2 the presence of water vapor in the room slightly reduces the proportions of oxygen and nitrogen. To (cid:28)nd the mole fraction (i.e. percent by volume) of water vapor, use the steam table, Properties of Saturated Water by temperature and obtain the saturation pressure for water at 72◦F. T =72◦F →P =0.39psia sat@72◦F Applying the de(cid:28)nition or Relative Humidity, the partial pressure of water vapor is 80% of the saturation pressure. p φ= w →p =φp =(0.8)(0.39psia)=0.312psia p w sat sat Since the air in the room is at atmospheric pressure, the mole fraction of water vapor can be determined by taking the ratio of the partial pressure of water vapor to the total pressure. p 0.312psia x = w = =0.021 h2o p 14.7psia t This result implies that 2.1% of the moles in the room are water vapor. The remaining 97.9% is dry air and will exist in the expected 21% oxygen / 79% nitrogen proportions mentioned earlier. Calculate the mole fractions for oxygen and nitrogen accounting for the presence of water. x =(0.979)(0.21)=0.206 O2 x =(0.979)(0.79)=0.773 N2 Finally, determine the Mass Fraction of nitrogen by multiplying by the atomic weight, and dividing by the sum of the mass of each constituent in the air. Use the Periodic Table as needed to look up atomic weights of oxygen, nitrogen, and water. x M (0.773)(28) y = N2 N2 = =0.756 N2 x M +x M +x M (0.773)(28)+(0.206)(32)+(0.021)(18) N2 N2 O2 O2 h2o h2o Answer B 155

problems.statement: A dry product analysis of the flue gas after butane is burned in air shows that the products contain 5.6% O and
problems.solution: air shows that the products contain 5.6% O and 10.2% CO . How 2 2 much excess air was in the reaction? A. 8% B. 26% C. 34% D. 40% Start by writing the stoichiometric reaction for butane burned in air. The formula shown in the table Combustion Reactions of Common Fuel Constituents omits nitrogen because it does not participate in the combustion process, however nitrogen does appear in the dry product analysis. Water is not included in the dry product analysis. Include 3.77 molecules of nitrogren for every molecule of oxygen as this is the naturally occurring ratio in atmospheric air. Note the air to fuel ratio on a volume or molar basis is 6.5 when there is no excess air. C H +6.5(O +3.77N )→4CO +5H O+24.5N (stoichiometric) 4 10 2 2 2 2 2 Write another reaction for the excess air scenario and intially include variables before each product or reactant to account for unknown values. Also include an oxygen product since excess O will be provided and remain unburned. 2 aC H +b(O +3.77N )→cCO +dH O+eO +fN 4 10 2 2 2 2 2 2 Consider the products, ignoring water as it is omitted from the dry product analysis, and use the percentages given as the number of moles of each product. Since the dry products must add to 100%, calculate the moles of nitrogen. c=10.2 e=5.6 c+e+f =100 f =100−c−e=100−10.2−5.6=84.2 Balance the carbon. 4a=c c 10.2 a= = =2.55 4 4 Balance the hydrogen. 10a=2d 308 d=5a=5(2.55)=12.75 Balance the oxygen. 2b=2c+d+2e 2c+d+2e 2(10.2)+12.75+2(5.6) b= = =22.2 2 2 Check the nitrogren balance. Expect a small amount of error due to rounding. 2(3.77)b=2f 2(3.77)(22.2)=2(84.2) 167.4≈168.4(cid:8) Find the air to fuel ratio on a molar basis for the reaction with excess air. air b 22.2 = = =8.71 fuel a 2.55 Compare the air to fuel ratio obtained with the original stoichiometric reaction and calculate the excess as a percentage. 8.71−6.5 excessair = =34% 6.5 Answer C 309

problems.statement: Machine A produces a sound pressure of 58dB, and machine B pro- duces a sound pressure of 52dB. What is the combined sound pres- sure?
problems.solution: duces a sound pressure of 52dB. What is the combined sound pres- sure? A. 53dB B. 58dB C. 59dB D. 61dB RefertothetableforCombiningTwoSoundLevels. Notethatwhenthedi(cid:27)erencebetweenthedB levels of the two sources is between 5 and 9, the number of dB to be added to the highest source is 1dB. Since Machine A is the higher source, add 1dB to its sound pressure level. 58dB+1dB =59dB Answer C

problems.statement: The background noise in an offce has a sound pressure level of 38dB. An overhead fan coil unit with a sound pressure level of 43dB turns on. What is the combind sound pressure level?
problems.solution: 38dB. An overhead fan coil unit with a sound pressure level of 43dB turns on. What is the combind sound pressure level? A. 39dB B. 44dB C. 46dB D. 52dB Refer to the table for Combining Two Sound Levels. Note that when the di(cid:27)erence between the dB levels of two sources is between 5 and 9, the number of dB to be added to the highest source is 1dB. Combine the two sources. 43dB+1dB =44dB Answer B 328

problems.statement: Four identical fans produce a combined sound pressure level of 90dBA as measured at a point that is equidistant from each fan. What is the sound pressure level if three fans are shut down?
problems.solution: 90dBA as measured at a point that is equidistant from each fan. What is the sound pressure level if three fans are shut down? A. 70dB B. 80dB C. 84dB D. 87dB Refer to the table for Combining Two Sound Levels and note that when the di(cid:27)erence between two source’s sound levels is 0dB, the number of decibels to be added to the highest level is 3dB. In this situation, sources are being removed as fans are shut down, but the same principle applies in reverse. To be clear, the table does not o(cid:27)er a way to add 3 or more sources together directly, therefore it is necessary to (cid:28)nd an approach that allows adding the sources in pairs only. First, imagine shutting 2 of the 4 fans down. One pair of fans may be treated as one source and the other pair treated as a second source. The sound pressure level will be reduced by 3dB when have the sources are removed. This reasoning can be con(cid:28)rmed by imagining re-enabling the 2 fans, thereby combining two pairs of sources, and adding back the 3dB. 90dB−3dB =87dB Next imagine shutting down one of the two remaining fans, thereby removing half the sources. By the same reasoning, another 3dB reduction will be observed. 87dB−3dB =84dB Again, sense check this answer by adding two 84dB sources together to get 87dB, then adding the two pair of sources to get back to 90dB. Answer C

problems.statement: The background noise in a factory prior to any equipment turning on has a sound pressure level of 40dB. Once the equipment is oper- ating, the sound pressure level is 46dB. What is the sound pressure level attributable to the machinery only? 58
problems.solution: on has a sound pressure level of 40dB. Once the equipment is oper- ating, the sound pressure level is 46dB. What is the sound pressure level attributable to the machinery only? A. 6dB B. 41dB C. 43dB D. 45dB RefertothetableforCombiningTwoSoundLevels. Theprocessforcombiningsoundlevelsinvolves addingupto3dB tothehighest source,dependingonthedi(cid:27)erenceinsoundlevelbetweenthetwo sources. In this case, it is not immediately clear whether the background noise or the machinery is theloudersource. Sincethemostthatcouldbeaddedtoarriveatthecombinedsoundpressurelevel 329 is 3dB, it can be inferred that the background noise is not as loud at the machinery, as combining two 40dB sources would result in a combined level of only 43dB. Therefore, the machinery must be louder. Test values for the machinery between 41dB and 45dB. SPL =41dB →Difference=1dB →CombinedSPL=41dB+3dB =44dB (cid:54)=46dB machinery SPL =42dB →Difference=2dB →CombinedSPL=42dB+2dB =44dB (cid:54)=46dB machinery SPL =43dB →Difference=3dB →CombinedSPL=43dB+2dB =45dB (cid:54)=46dB machinery SPL =44dB →Difference=4dB →CombinedSPL=44dB+2dB =46dB =46dB machinery SPL =45dB →Difference=5dB →CombinedSPL=45dB+1dB =46dB =46dB machinery Note there are two viable answer choices, 44dB and 45dB. However, only 45dB is an answer choice. Answer D

problems.statement: The handle of a large manual valve is secured with a square bolt pattern, as shown. The handle is 18in long and the force applied to the end of the handle during valve opration is 30lb . The bolts f have a nominal diameter of 1in and a cross-sectional area of .03in2. 4 When the valve is being closed, which statement can be made about the shear stress experienced by the bolts?

choices.A: Bolts A and B experience higher shear stress than bolts C and D.
choices.B: Bolts C and D experience higher shear stress than bolts A and B.
choices.C: Bolts A and C experience higher shear stress than bolts B and D.
choices.D: Bolts B and D experience higher shear stress than bolts A and C.

problems.solution: pattern, as shown. The handle is 18in long and the force applied to the end of the handle during valve opration is 30lb . The bolts f have a nominal diameter of 1in and a cross-sectional area of .03in2. 4 When the valve is being closed, which statement can be made about the shear stress experienced by the bolts? A. Bolts A and B experience higher shear stress than bolts C and D. B. Bolts C and D experience higher shear stress than bolts A and B. C. Bolts A and C experience higher shear stress than bolts B and D. D. Bolts B and D experience higher shear stress than bolts A and C. This is a qualitative problem, so there is no need to quantify the loads applied to the bolts. Since allboltshavethesamediameterandcross-sectionalarea,themaximumshearstresswillbeadirect outcome of the resultant force experienced due to the applied load and moment. Since the applied load is downward, the reaction forces on the bolts from the applied load are all upward. Since the load is applied a distance away from the bolts, it produces a clockwise moment, generating reaction forces perpendicular to lines drawn through each bolt and the center of the bolt pattern. In othwer words, the forces resulting from the moment are tangent to a circle drawn through all four bolts. 336 Adding the vectors for the reaction forces from the applied load and the moment, notice the directions of the reaction forces partially o(cid:27)set for bolts A and B. However, the forces are partially additiveforboltsCandD.Therefore,boltsCandDhavelargerresultantreactionforcesandmore shear stress than bolts A and B. Answer B

problems.statement: A company is considering whether to invest in a piece of equipment that will have an initial cost of $100,000 and will produce $50,000 per year in revenue for the next 3 years before maintenance costs and taxes. Maintenence will cost $5000 per year. The tax rate is 30% and straight line depreciation is used for the equipment. The equipment has a salvage value of $20,000 to be realized at the end of year 3. What is the present value of the initiaitve using a 10% rate of return? 62
problems.solution: that will have an initial cost of $100,000 and will produce $50,000 per year in revenue for the next 3 years before maintenance costs and taxes. What is the present value of the initiaitve using a 10% rate of return? A. -$62K B. $13K C. $46K D. $211K Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram and make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. Year 0: -$100K Years 1 & 2: Revenue = 50K and Cost = 5K. The pro(cid:28)t before tax is 45K. The taxes may be addressed separately. Year 3: The cash (cid:29)ow is the same as for years 1 & 2 except for an additional $20K in salvage value which will be included a separate one-o(cid:27) future payment. For tax purposes, the pro(cid:28)t which is considered taxable is reduced by the depreciation for that year even though the cash was actually spent in year 0. For straight line depreciation, the depreciated amount for years 1, 2, and 3 is the same. $100,000−$20,000 D = =$26,667peryear 3years Determine the pro(cid:28)t for tax purposes. Profit=Revenue−(Expenses+Depreciation) Profit=$50,000−($5,000+$26,667)=$18,333 Apply the tax rate to determine the tax liability. Tax=($18,333)(0.3)=$5,500 Subtract the tax from the before-tax pro(cid:28)t (excluding depreciation) to determine the after-tax pro(cid:28)t. This is the net cash (cid:29)ow for years 1 & 2. AftertaxProfit=$45,000−$5,500=$39,500 For year 3, the salvage value, which is not subject to taxes, will be handled as a separate future payment in our present value calculation. In other words, the $38,500 will be considered part of an annualized cash (cid:29)ow with a 3 year period. 340 Calculate the present value. Use the Economic Factor Tables to look up the cash (cid:29)ow factors for P/A and P/F based on an interest rate of 10%. PV =−$100,000+($39,500)(P/A,10%,3)+($20,000)(P/F,10%,3) PV =−$100,000+($39,500)(2.4

problems.statement: A company purchases a factory for $1M. The factory will generate a gross annual revenue of $140K and incur annual expenses of $60K. The facility has an expected life of 20 years and no salvage value. Assuming straight-line depreciation and a 30% e(cid:27)ective tax rate, what is the after tax rate of return if the company sells the factory for $800K and exits the investment after 5 years? Ignore tax implications of capital losses at the time of sale.
problems.solution: a gross annual revenue of $140K and incur annual expenses of $60K. The facility has an expected life of 20 years and no salvage value. Assuming straight-line depreciation and a 30% e(cid:27)ective tax rate, what is the after tax rate of return if the company sells the factory for $800K and exits the investment after 5 years? Ignore tax implications of capital losses at the time of sale. A. 1.5% B. 3.5% C. 5.5% D. 7.5% Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. The cash (cid:29)ow in year 0 is -$1M. The cash (cid:29)ow in year 5 is +$800K in addition to the net pro(cid:28)t after tax. To determine the net pro(cid:28)t after tax for years 1 through 5, start by subtracting the expenses from the revenue. ProfitBeforeTax=Revenue−Expenses=$140K−$60K =$80K Becausethereisdepreciation,thetaxesarenotassessedagainstthe$80K.Thetaxableamounnt is reduced by the depreciation. Essentially, depreciation is applied as an expense even though the cash left the business in year 0. For straight line deprecation of $1M of initial cost spread over 20 years: $1,000,000 D = =$50K 20 The taxable income is therefore: TaxableIncome=$80K−$50K =$30K The tax liability based on a 30% tax rate is: Tax=(0.3)($30K)=$9K Determine the Net Pro(cid:28)t After Tax: 341 NetProfitAfterTax=ProfitBeforeTax−Tax=$80K−$9K =$71K Write an expression for the present value of the investment accounting for the initial cost, the net pro(cid:28)t after tax for years 1 through 5, and the salvage value in year 5. Set the present value equal to zero. By de(cid:28)nition, the interest rate that gives the investment a present value of zero is the rate of return. PV =−$1,000,000+$71,000(P/A,i,5)+$800,000(P/F,i,5)=0 The math required for a direct solution becomes complex, so it is faster and equally reliable to use trial an error and interpolation to determine the interest rate. Guesses are best made in the vicinity of the answer choices with a strong preference for values o(cid:27)ered in the Economic

problems.statement: A project has an initial cost of $200K and generates $30K per year in net profits. After 15 years there is a salvage value of $40K. What is the return on investment for the project?
problems.solution: year in net pro(cid:28)ts. After 15 years there is a salvage value of $40K. What is the return on investment for the project? A. 13.3% B. 14.2% C. 15.1% D. 16.0% Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. For year 0 there is an initial cost of -$200K. For years 1 through 15 there is an annual pro(cid:28)t of $30K. For year 15 there is a salvage value of $40K. Write an expressions for the present value of the project. Set the present value equal to zero. Thereturnoninvestment(ROI)istheinterestratethatgivestheinitiativeapresentvalueofzero. PV =−$200,000+$30,000(P/A,i%,15)+$40,000(P/F,i%,15)=0 Themathinvolvedinsolvingfortheinterestrateischallengingandtimeconsuming,thereforea trialanderrormethodisoftenthebestwayforwardfromthispoint. Onewaytogetanapproximate answer to look at the answer choices and notice they are all between 12% and 18%. Another way istonoticethattheonetimesalvagevalueismuchlesssigni(cid:28)cant(thoughnotnegligible)thanthe annual pro(cid:28)t, so it can be temporarily ignored as a (cid:28)rst pass. PV =−$200,000+$30,000(P/A,i%,15)≈0 $200K (P/A,i%,15)≈ ≈6.7 $30K Refering to the i = 12% Factor Table, the value for (P/A,i%,15) is 6.8109 which is slightly higher than 6.7. Since P/A gets smaller as interest rates increase, the ROI must be greater than 12%. Also, the salvage value (previously ignored) is positive and drives the present value up which meansfuturecash(cid:29)owswillneedtobediscountedslightlymoreandtheinterestrateisgoingtobe greater than 12%. This is about as far as qualitative judgment goes. To arrive at a precice answer, it is necessary to (cid:28)nd the PV using interest rates of 12% and 18%, then interpolate. PV =−$200,000+$30,000(P/A,12%,15)+$40,000(P/F,12%,15) i=12 343 PV =−$200,000+$30,000(6.8109)+$40,000(0.1827)=$11,635>0 i=12 Therefore, ROI >12% PV =−$200,000+$30,000(P/A,18%,15)+$40,000(P/F,18%,15) i=18 PV =−$200,000+$30,000(5.0916)+$40,000(0.0835)=−$43,912<0 i=18 Therefore, ROI <18% Interpolate and solve for i.

problems.statement: A $100,000 investment today pays back $10,000 in years 1 through 9 and $100,000 in year 10. What is the rate of return? 63
problems.solution: 9 and $100,000 in year 10. What is the rate of return? A. 7.8% B. 8.6% C. 9.4% D. 10.3% The rate of return is the interest rate that makes the present value zero. Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. There is an initial outlay of $100,000 in year zero (negative). In years 1-9 there is a return of $10,000 per year. In year 10, there is a return of the original $100,000 principle. Write an expressions for the present value and set it equal to zero. PV =−$100,000+$10,000(P/A,i,9)+$100,000(P/F,i,10%)=0 Direct solutions are challenging when the interest rate is unknown. The fastest approach is to guess values for i in close proximity to the answer choices. It is also possible to try the answer choices by trial and error; however, since only certain integer percentages can be found in the 344 Economic Factor Tables, this would require using the Economic Factor Conversions to derive the cash (cid:29)ow factors. This solution will describe the process without testing answer choices. Assume i=8%. PV =−$100,000+$10,000(6.2469)+$100,000(0.4632)=$8,789>0 Sincethepresentvalueisgreater thanzero,theinterestratemustbehigher,therebydiscounting the future (positive) cash (cid:29)ows more and reducing the present value. Assume i=10%. PV =−$100,000+$10,000(5.7590)+$100,000(0.3855)=−$3,860<0 Since the present value is less than zero, the interest rate must be lower, thereby discounting the future (positive) cash (cid:29)ows less and increasing the present value. Looking at the answer choices, it is possible at this stage to eliminate choices A and D as they are outside the range of 8-10% which is now known to contain the interest rate that drives the present to zero. Furthermore, it is possible to infer that the interest rate will be closer to 10% than 8% since the resulting present value obtained for i=10% was closer to zero. If time allows, interpolate and solve for i. i[%] PV [$] 8 8,789 i 0 10 −3,860 i−8 0−8,789 = =0.695 10−8 −3,860−8,789 i−8=1.39 i=9.4

In years 1 through 6, there is an annual revenue of $15,000. In years 7-12, there is an annual revenue of $20,000. Rather than handle the (6) additional $5,000 cash (cid:29)ows in years 7-12 as individual future cash (cid:29)ows, it is faster to assume and annual revenue of $20,000 for the entire 12 years, then compensate foroverstatingtherevenueinyears1-6bysubtracting $5,000peryearover6yearsfromtheoutset. This can be handled as an annualized cash (cid:29)ow over 6 years. The present value can be determined with the following expression. PV =−$100,000+($20,000)(P/A,6%,12)−($5,000)(P/A,6%,6) Usethe6%FactorTabletolookupthecash(cid:29)owfactorsneededtotranslatethecash(cid:29)owsinto present value. Solve for the present value. PV =−$100,000+($20,000)(8.3838)−($5,000)(4.9173)=$43,090 Answer A 352
