problems.solution: temper the fresh air intake during winter operation. The heating coil is sized for 80,000 Btu/hr. The outside conditions are 20(cid:176)F and 50% relative humidity. What is the humidity ratio after heating? A. 0.001lbw lbda B. 0.002lbw lbda C. 0.003lbw lbda D. 0.004lbw lbda The humidity ratio after heating is the same as the humidity ratio before heating because the heating process is purely sensible only, i.e. there is no moisture being added or removed by the heating coil. Therefore, the problem can be distilled down to (cid:28)nding the humidity ratio of the outside air based on known temperature and relative humidity. Although the state is fully de(cid:28)ned, the Psychrometric Chart only goes down to 35◦F and no low temperature psychrometric chart is provided in the Reference Handbook. As an alternative, search for the table Thermodynamic Properties of Moist Air and note the humidity ratio at saturation (100% relative humidity): lb @T =20◦F →ω =0.002153 w s lb da The humidity ratio at any other level of humidity can be calculated by using the Degree of Saturation, µ. As an approximation, it is acceptable to assume the degree of saturation is roughly equivalent to the relative humidity. This is not strictly true but works well in a pinch. Solve for the humidity ratio using µ. φ≈µ≈50% ω µ= → ω =µω ω s s (cid:18) (cid:19) lb lb ω =(.5) 0.002153 w =0.00108 w lb lb da da Answer A

problems.statement: A fan supplies air through a 12in by 18in rectangular sheet metal duct with 1in fiberglass insulation. The duct run is 15ft long. Local octave band measurements of the sound power level for the fan are 125Hz, 95dB; 250Hz, 94dB; 500Hz, 92dB; 1000Hz, 90dB; 2000Hz, 85dB; 4000Hz, 69dB. What is the expected sound power level for the 1000Hz octave band at the end of the duct run?
problems.solution: duct with 1in (cid:28)berglass insulation. The duct run is 15ft long. Local octave band measurements of the sound power level for the fan are 125Hz, 95dB; 250Hz, 94dB; 500Hz, 92dB; 1000Hz, 90dB; 2000Hz, 85dB; 4000Hz, 69dB. What is the expected sound power level for the 1000Hz octave band at the end of the duct run? A. 35dB B. 52dB C. 69dB D. 86dB Refer to the table Insertion Loss for Rectangular Sheet Metal Ducts with 1 in. Fiberglass Lining. Look up dimensions 12in by 18in and note the insertion loss for the 1000Hz octave band is 3.7dB. ft Multiply the loss per foot times the length of the duct to obtain the total dB reduction. (cid:18) (cid:19) dB 3.7 (15ft)=55.5dB ft Subtract the dB reduction from the measured sound power level for the 1000Hz octave band to obtain the (cid:28)nal sound power level with the indulated duct inserted. 90dB−55.5dB =34.5dB Answer A

problems.statement: A normal shock wave in air has a Mach number of 3. The pressure upstream is 1atm, what is the pressure downsteam?

In both cases, the subscript 1 is used to represent the upstream conditions and the subscript 2 is used to represent the downstream conditions. Using the table, for M =3, (cid:28)nd the corresponding pressure ratio. 1 P 2 =10.3333 P 1 363 Since the upstream pressure is known, substitute for P and convert from atm to psi. 1 (cid:18) (cid:19) 14.7psi P =10.3333(P )=10.3333(1atm) =152psi 2 1 atm Alternatively, since the table values have been generated for convenience, it is also valid to use the underlying formulas to solve for the pressure ratio. The ratio of speci(cid:28)c heats, k, is assumed to be 1.4. problems.statement: A company purchases a factory for $1M with a salvage value of $300Kin15years. Operationsandmaintenancecostsare$40K/year. At an interest rate of 8%, what is the equivalent uniform annual cost of the factory over the next 15 years? 69
problems.solution: $300Kin15years. Operationsandmaintenancecostsare$40K/year. At an interest rate of 8%, what is the equivalent uniform annual cost of the factory over the next 15 years? A. $70K B. $90K C. $150K D. $170K Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. Since the problem is asking for EUAC, this solution treats costs as positive. For year 0 there is a payment for the original purchase of $1M. For years 1 through 15 there is an annualized payment for operation and maintenance of $40K per year. For year 15 there is a positive cash (cid:29)ow of $300K for the salvage value which partially o(cid:27)sets the costs. Since the O&M cost in years 1 through 15 is already annualized, there is no need for further manipulation. The initial cost and the salvage value need to be transformed into annualized cash (cid:29)ows and added to the O&M. Use the Factor Table for 8% to look up the required cash (cid:29)ow factors. Solve for the EUAC. EUAC =$40,000+$1,000,000(A/P,8%,15)−$300,000(A/F,8%,15) EUAC =$40,000+$116,800−11,040=$145,760 Answer C 364

The minimum present value is the best choice. problems.statement: A group of machines are maintained under a contract which costs $50,000 this year. The contract cost will inrease by $1000 per year over the next 10 years. What is the present value of the entire 10 years of maintenance using an e(cid:27)ctive annual interest rate of 12%.
problems.solution: $50,000 this year. The contract cost will inrease by $1000 per year over the next 10 years. What is the present value of the entire 10 years of maintenance using an e(cid:27)ctive annual interest rate of 12%. A. $303,000 B. $336,000 C. $352,000 D. $370,000 The present value can be represented as the sum of two cash (cid:29)ows: an recurring annunal cost of $50,000 and a uniform gradient of $1,000. The gradient has a value of zero in the (cid:28)rst year, such that the (cid:28)rst year cost is $50,000, second year is $51,000, third year is $52,000, etc. There is no initialcostre(cid:29)ectedinyear0asitiscustomaryinengineeringeconomicstore(cid:29)ectcoststhatoccur throughout the year at the end of the year. Write an expression for the present value. PV =A(P/A,12%,10)+G(P/G,12%,10) Use the 12% Factor Table to look up the cash (cid:29)ow factors needed to translate the cash (cid:29)ows into present value. Solve for the present value. PV =($50,000)(5.6502)+($1,000)(20.2541)=$302,764 Answer A

problems.statement: A pump delivers 200 GPM of water at 130 feet of total dynamic head, operating from 7am-7pm Monday through Friday. The pump is 80% effcient and the motor is 93% effcient. What is the annual cost of operation at $0.13 per kWh?
problems.solution: head, operating from 7am-7pm Monday through Friday. The pump is 80% e(cid:30)cient and the motor is 93% e(cid:30)cient. What is the annual cost of operation at $0.13 per kWh? A. $2670 B. $3490 C. $3750 D. $5030 The cost is a function of electrical power and time, and electrical power is a function of hydraulic horsepower (aka water horsepower i.e. whp) and e(cid:30)ciency. Start by calculating the water horse- power based on volume (cid:29)ow rate and feet of head provided by the pump: Q∆h (200)(130) whp= = =6.566hp 3960 3960 366 Note the volume and head units must be in GPM and ft, respectively, to use this (cid:16)rule of thumb(cid:17) equation. Therefore units need not be shown, provided they are con(cid:28)rmed to be correct prior to use. Recall that brake horsepower, bhp, depends on water horsepower, whp, and the e(cid:30)ciency of the pump, η . Similarly, the electrical power, W˙ ,depends on brake horsepower, bhp, and motor p e(cid:30)ciency, η . m whp bhp= η p bhp W˙ = η m Put these together, substitute, solve, and convert to KW: (cid:18) (cid:19) whp 6.566hp .746KW W˙ = = =6.58KW η η (.8)(.93) 1hp p m To (cid:28)nd the annual cost, multiply by time and the unit rate of electricity: (cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19)(cid:18) (cid:19) 12hrs 5days 52wks $0.13 Cost=(6.58KW) =$2669peryear day wk yr KWH Answer A

problems.statement: A 7.5hp single phase 230V motor drawing 40A at full load is lo- cated 50meters from the voltage source. The motor is wired with 8AWG wire which has a cross sectional area of 16,509circularmils (1circularmil = 5.066×10−10m2) and a resistivity of 1.724×10−8Ω·m. What is the percent voltage drop for the wiring in the circuit? 70
problems.solution: cated 50meters from the voltage source. The motor is wired with 8AWG wire which has a cross sectional area of 16,509circularmils (1circularmil = 5.066×10−10m2) and a resistivity of 1.724×10−8Ω·m. What is the percent voltage drop for the wiring in the circuit? A. 1% B. 2% C. 3% D. 4% The electrical resistance attributable to the wiring is the result of copper’s resistivity, which is an intrinsic material property, and the length and gauge of the wire. The formula for resistivity can be rearranged to solve for the total resistance. RA ρ= L ρL R= A Since the motor is 50ft from the voltage source, a su(cid:30)cient length of wire must be provided to make a round trip from the source to the load and back. Therefore, the total length is given by: 367 L=2(50m)=100m Determine the resistance. ρL (cid:0) 1.724×10−8Ω·m (cid:1) (100m) R= = =0.206Ω A (16,509cmil) (cid:0) 5.066×10−10 m2 (cid:1) cmil Find the voltage drop due to the wire by applying Ohm’s Law. V =IR=(40A)(0.206Ω)=8.25V drop Determine the percentage voltage drop by dividing by the nominal voltage of the source. V 8.25V drop = =0.036=3.6% V 230V source Answer D

problems.statement: 1000cfm of 350◦F, 80psia air is supplied by an air compressor. What is the standard volume flow rate (SCFM)?
problems.solution: is the standard volume (cid:29)ow rate (SCFM)? A. 100cfm B. 300cfm C. 3500cfm D. 8500cfm Since the air being supplied is at an elevated temperature and pressure, it is necessary to make Temperature and Altitude Corrections for Air. This can be achieved using the factors in the table; however,thepressureisgiveninpsiaratherthanasanelevation,thereforeitismorestraightforward to multiply by the ratio of the pressure as compared to standard atmosphereic pressure. Similarly, a temperature ratio multiplier is also convenient to use, provided absolute temperature units are used. Standard temperature and pressure may be considered 60◦F and 14.7psia. Intermsofqualitativeexpectations,ahigherthanstandardpressureimpliesthestandardCFM (SCFM) will be higher than the actual CFM. A higher than standard temperature implies the standard CFM will be lower than the actual CFM. In concept, SCFM is attempt to explain how much volume the compressor would move if pumping air at STP. (cid:18) 80psia (cid:19)(cid:18) 60◦F +460◦R (cid:19) SCFM =(1000cfm) =3,493cfm 14.7psia 350◦F +460◦R Answer C 368

problems.statement: The piston in the master cylinder of a car with a hydraulic clutch system has a diameter that is double the diameter of the slave cylinder piston. If the driver presses the clutch pedal with an input force of 50N, what is the reaction force at the slave cylinder piston?
problems.solution: system has a diameter that is double the diameter of the slave cylinder piston. If the driver presses the clutch pedal with an input force of 50N, what is the reaction force at the slave cylinder piston? A. 13N B. 25N C. 100N D. 200N The key principle for hydraulics problems is that the pressure is equal throughout the system. The force applied and the reaction force may vary according to the diameter/area, but the pressure is equal. Start this problem by recognizing that the pressure applied at the master cylinder is equal to the pressure exerted on the slave cylinder. P =P m s Next apply the de(cid:28)nition of pressure as a force applied over an area. Substitude for the both sides of the equation. F P = A F F m = s A A m s Since the applied force at the master cyclinder is known, isolate the reaction force F . s (cid:18) (cid:19) A F =F s s m A m Theratiooftheareasisathesquareoftheratioofthediameters. Themastercyclinderdiameter was given as double the slave cylinder diameter. A (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)2 s = =0.25 A 2 m Solve for the reaction force at the slave cylinder. F =(50N)(0.25)=12.5N s Answer A 369

problems.statement: A cooling tower cools 150gpm of condenser water maintaining a 15◦F delta T. A. 8,100CFM B. 12,500CFM C. 69,400CFM D. 119,700CFM The quantity of heat removed from the air is added to the condenser water. Therefore: Q =Q a w The sensible heating of the condenser water can be determined using the rule of thumb, where the (cid:29)ow rate and di(cid:27)erential temperature are known: Btu Q =500gpm∆T =500(150)(15)=1,125,000 w hr The heat removed from air in a cooling tower is driven by a substantial amount of evaporation; therefore, use the rule of thumb for total cooling of air (including both sensible and latent) should be used: Q =4.5cfm∆h a where the volume (cid:29)ow rate of air is unknown. To specify the change in enthalpy, use the Psychrometric Chart to look up the enthalpy values for entering and leaving conditions, both of which are fully de(cid:28)ned. Use of the High Temperature psychrometric chart is required for State 2. 371 State 1: Entering Air State 2: Leaving Air T =90◦F T =105◦F 1,db 2,db T =75◦F T 1,wb 2,wb φ φ =80% 1 2 h =38.4Btu h =69.4Btu 1 lb 2 lb Calculate the change in enthalpy: Btu Btu Btu ∆h=h −h =69.4 −38.4 =31 2 1 lb lb lb Use the fact that Q =Q to solve for the unknown volume (cid:29)ow rate. Note that by using the a w rule of thumb selected, the units will automatically come out in cfm provided the enthalpy is in Btu and the heat transfer is in Btu. lb hr 1,125,000 cfm= =8065cfm (4.5)(31) Answer A

problems.statement: Methane is burned with 20% excess air. What is the air-to-fuel ratio? lb lb lb lb
problems.solution: ratio? A. 14lb lb B. 17lb lb C. 21lb lb D. 24lb lb Look up the table Combustion Reactions of Common Fuel Constituents and (cid:28)nd the combustion reaction for methane burned stoichiometrically in air. Include 3.76 nitrogen molecules for every 1 oxygen molecule since nitrogen has been omitted in the table. Nitrogen does not participate in the reaction; however, it does contribute mass to the air and thus impacts the air-to-fuel ratio. CH +2O +2(3.76)N →CO +2H O+2(3.76)N 4 2 2 2 2 2 Add 20% excess air. Air-to-fuel ratio applies only to the reactants, so it is not necessary to balance the reaction and write out the product side. CH +1.2[2O +2(3.76)N ]→products 4 2 2 CH +(2.4)O +(9.02)N →products 4 2 2 Calculate the air-to-fuel ratio by multiplying the number of moles of each constituent times the atomicweightanddividingtheairmassbythefuelmass. UsethePeriodicTabletolookupatomic weights as needed. m n M +n M (2.4)(32)+(9.02)(28) lb air = O2 O2 N2 N2 = =20.6 m n M (1)(16) lb fuel CH4 CH4 Alternatively, intheCombustionReactionstable, noticetheheadingforStoichiometricOxygen and Air Requirements. Increase this value by 20% to account for excess air. lb (cid:18) (cid:19) lb lb (1.2) 17.24 =20.7 lb lb Answer C 375

problems.statement: What is the maximum theoretical effciency of a power cycle op- erating with a minimum temperature of 70◦F and a maximum temperature of 900◦F? 73

Find the chart for Refrigerant 134a. Followingahorizontallineatapressureof20psiatotherightmostedgeofthesaturationcurve, corresponding to a saturated vapor condition. Continue horizontally to the right to account for the additional 20◦F of superheat. Note that to the right of the saturation curve, lines of constant temperature are curved downward (nearly vertical). This is the compressor entering condition, typically noted as State 1 of the refrigeration cycle. Once the state point has been identi(cid:28)ed, read o(cid:27) the entropy from the chart. Note that lines of constant entropy run roughly southwest/northeast on the diagram. Btu s≈0.235 lb◦F Answer C 384

problems.statement: In a counterflow heat exchanger, the cold fluid increases in temper- ature from 50◦F to 100◦F and the warm fluid is cooled from 180◦F to 145◦F. What is the logarithmic mean temperature di(cid:27)erence?
problems.solution: ature from 50◦F to 100◦F and the warm (cid:29)uid is cooled from 180◦F to 145◦F. What is the logarithmic mean temperature di(cid:27)erence? A. 45◦F B. 87◦F C. 115◦F D. 119◦F It is valid to use the Reference Handbook formula for Log Mean Temperature Di(cid:27)erence. There is also a generalized version of the LMTD equation not provided in the reference handbook which is easier to remember and applies to both Counter(cid:29)ow and Parallel Flow heat exchangers, provided the (cid:29)ow directions are drawn out (cid:28)rst. Note the opposite direction for the arrows for a counter(cid:29)ow heat exchanger. ColdFluid: 50◦F −→100◦F HotFluid: 145◦F ←−180◦F De(cid:28)ne one physical side of the heat exchanger as ’A’ and the other side as ’B’ and determine the respective temperature di(cid:27)erences. Conveniently, the assignment of labels A and B turns out to be arbitrary. However, the direction of the (cid:29)ows is critical. ∆T =145◦F −50◦F =95◦F A ∆T =180◦F −100◦F =80◦F B Use the formula below to calculate the log mean temperature di(cid:27)erence. 390 ∆T −∆T LMTD = A B (cid:16) (cid:17) ln ∆TA ∆TB 95◦F −80◦F LMTD = =87.3◦F ln (cid:0)95◦F(cid:1) 80◦F Answer B

problems.statement: A 75KVA transformer with 99% effciency is located in an electrical room at70◦F. The transformer’s enclosure is a floor standing box with dimensions of 3ft height, 2ft width, 3ft depth. The exposed surfaces have an average temperature of 85◦F. The load is resistive and uses 60% of the transformer’s capacity. What is the overall heat transfer coeffcient? hr·ft2·◦F hr·ft2·◦F hr·ft2·◦F hr·ft2·◦F
problems.solution: room at70◦F. The transformer’s enclosure is a (cid:29)oor standing box with dimensions of 3ft height, 2ft width, 3ft depth. The exposed surfaces have an average temperature of 85◦F. The load is resistive and uses 60% of the transformer’s capacity. What is the overall heat transfer coe(cid:30)cient? A. 1.9 Btu hr·ft2·◦F B. 2.4 Btu hr·ft2·◦F C. 2.8 Btu hr·ft2·◦F D. 4.7 Btu hr·ft2·◦F The Overall Heat Transfer Coe(cid:30)cient is given by: Q˙ Q˙ =UA∆T →U = A∆T The surface area for the (cid:29)oor standing enclosure includes 5 faces: the top and 4 sides. Note there is no base area to include in the surface area. A=(2)[(3ft)(3ft)]+(3)[(2ft)(3ft)]=36ft2 The temperature di(cid:27)erential is the di(cid:27)erence between the exposed surfaces of the enclosure and the ambient temperature in the electrical room. ∆T =85◦F −70◦F =15◦F The heat produced by the transformer depends on the demand, e(cid:30)ciency, and power factor. problems.statement: Which of the following is not a reason for pumps to be placed in parallel?
problems.solution: parallel? A. To increase pressure B. To increase volume (cid:29)ow rate C. To improve e(cid:30)ciency D. To add redundancy Examine each choice: A: When pumps are run in parallel, the di(cid:27)erential pressure across the pump set is equal, not greater. Therefore pumps would not be placed in parallel to increase pressure. They would need to be placed in series to add pressure. B: Parallel pumps provide additional volume at the same pressure. C: Parallel pumps may improve e(cid:30)ciency if using variable speed drives and programmed with the appropriate intent. D: Parallel pumps may provide redundancy if a su(cid:30)cient quantity are used. Answer A 417

problems.statement: The overall effciency of a gas turbine combined cycle is 55%. The gas turbine cycle standing alone has an effciency of 40%. What is the effciency of the Rankine cycle? 90
problems.solution: gas turbine cycle standing alone has an e(cid:30)ciency of 40%. What is the e(cid:30)ciency of the Rankine cycle? A. 9% B. 25% C. 75% D. 91% Refer to the Brayton Cycle for the gas turbine standing alone. Refer to the Combined Cycle when waste heat recovery is included. The heat recovery section can be modeled as a Rankine Cycle. The product of the losses from the Brayton Cycle and the losses from the Rankine Cycle equals the losses from the Combined Cycle. Solve for the e(cid:30)ciency of the Rankine Cycle. (1−η )(1−η )=(1−η ) Brayton Rankine Combined (1−0.4)(1−η )=(1−0.55) Rankine 421 (cid:18) (cid:19) 0.45 1−η = =0.75 Rankine 0.6 η =0.25 Rankine Another way to conceptualize this scenario is to notice that since the standalone Brayton Cycle has 40% e(cid:30)ciency, that implies 60% losses. Reason that to increase the overall e(cid:30)ciency of the combined cycle to from 40% to 55%, 15% of the wasted 60% must be converted into useful work, which is one fourth or 25% of the waste energy from the Brayton Cycle which is heat input to the Rankine Cycle. Answer B

problems.statement: A 7.5hp motor is 93% effcient. How much waste heat is generated due to losses? hr hr hr hr
problems.solution: due to losses? A. 810Btu hr B. 1340Btu hr C. 1440Btu hr D. 2580Btu hr The heat losses from the motor result from input electrical power that is not converted into brake horsepower. Since motors are rated in bhp, the mechanical output power is always equal to the nominal rating of the motor, in this case 7.5hp. Therefore, the losses do not come out of the 7.5hp, but rather, come out of the input electrical power leaving (precisely) the bhp left to be delivered. Calculate the electrical input power to the motor. bhp 7.5hp W˙ = = =8.065hp in η 0.93 motor The waste heat is the di(cid:27)erence between the input electrical power and the output shaft power. problems.solution: a winter day, the outside air is 18◦F and is heated to 68◦F using a hot water coil. Hot water is supplied at 165◦F and returned at 140◦F. What is the required (cid:29)ow rate of hot water? A. 0.4GPM B. 3GPM C. 22GPM D. 173GPM The air undergoes sensible heating. Use the sensible heating rule of thumb. Q =1.08CFM∆T s air The heat given up by the water can be determined with the sensible heating/cooling rule of thumb for water. Q =500GPM∆T w water The quantity of heat added to the air is supplied entirely by the hot water, therefore we can equate the two formulas. 1.08CFM∆T =500GPM∆T air water The volume (cid:29)ow rate of the hot water is the only unknown. Isolate, substitute, and solve for the GPM. 1.08CFM∆T 1.08(5000)(68−18) GPM = air = =21.6GPM 500∆T 500(165−140) water Note that no units need to be included when using these (cid:16)rules of thumb(cid:17) provided all input values are in the correct units in the (cid:28)rst place i.e. CFM, GPM, and Farenheit. Answer C 429

problems.statement: A plant room contains a generator with a noise level of 95dBA, an air compressor with a noise level of 88dBA, and a pump with a noise level of 77dBA. What is the overall noise level?
problems.solution: air compressor with a noise level of 88dBA, and a pump with a noise level of 77dBA. What is the overall noise level? A. 87dB B. 95dB C. 96dB D. 98dB Refer to the table for Combining Two Sound Levels. Note that when the di(cid:27)erence between the dB levels of two sources is between 5 and 9, the number of dB to be added to the highest source is 1dB. Also recall that only two sources may be added at once; the table provides no guidance on adding more than two sources. Start by combining the two loudest sources. 95dB+1dB =96dB Regarding the pump noise, recognize that the di(cid:27)erence between the noise levels between the pump and combined generator and compressor fall into the ’10dB or more’ range, therefore, there is no meaningful di(cid:27)erence to the combined level. Answer C

problems.statement: A data center developer has a choice between (1) building a $50M data center that will have a 30 year lifespan and cost $1M per year to maintain, and (2) building a $15M data center that will have a 10 year lifespan and cost $3M per year to maintain. If the interest rate is 8%, which alternative is superior?
problems.solution: data center that will have a 30 year lifespan and cost $1M per year to maintain, and (2) building a $15M data center that will have a 10 year lifespan and cost $3M per year to maintain. If the interest rate is 8%, which alternative is superior? A. Option 1 B. Option 2 C. Neither, the options are comparable. D. There is not enough information. Inengineeringeconomics, whenevaluatingalternatives, therearetwobasicapproaches: comparing the present value, and comparing the equivalent uniform annual cost (EUAC). When the two options have the same lice cycle, present value is a viable option. However, when the two options have di(cid:27)erent life cycles as is the case here, EUAC is the preferred approach. The maintenance costs are given as annual costs already, so the main task is annualizing the up front cost. Write expressions for the EUAC for both options. Use the i=8% Factor Table to retreive the cash (cid:29)ow factors. 430 EUAC =$50M(A/P,8%,30)+$1M =$50M(0.0888)+$1M =$5.44M 1 EUAC =$15M(A/P,8%,10)+$3M =$15M(0.1490)+$3M =$5.23M 2 Option 2 is superior. Answer B

problems.statement: A company with a tax rate of 30% makes a $50,000 one time pur- chase that drives $8,000 of annual revenue and carries $1200 of an- nual maintenance costs. The salvage value after 10 years is $5000. What is the present value of the investment over 10 years at a 6% interest rate? 94
problems.solution: chase that drives $8,000 of annual revenue and carries $1200 of an- nual maintenance costs. The salvage value after 10 years is $5000. What is the present value of the investment over 10 years at a 6% interest rate? A. −$12,200 B. −$6,000 C. $11,700 D. $52,800 Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. In Year 0, there is an initial payment of $50K (negative). In Years 1-10, there is a net pro(cid:28)t before tax of $8000−$1200 = $6800 and a net pro(cid:28)t after tax of $6800(1−0.3)=$4760. InYear10,thereisalsoafuturepaymentforthesalvagevalueof$5Kinadditiontotheafter-tax pro(cid:28)t. Note the tax rate is applied only to the annual pro(cid:28)ts and not to the initial cost or salvage value, and there is no reference to depreciation in the problem statement. Writeanexpressionforthepresentvalue. Usethei=6%FactorTabletoretreivethecash(cid:29)ow factors. PV =−$50,000+$4760(P/A,6%,10)+$5000(P/F,6%,10) PV =−$50,000+$4760(7.3601)+$5000(0.5584)=−$12,174 Answer A 431

problems.statement: A company pays $500K for a warehouse that it plans to hold for 15 years. The warehouse will save the company $5000 per month in shipping costs, boosting profit. Maintenance and taxes cost $10,000 per year. At the end of 15 years, what sale price is needed to realize an 8% rate of return?
problems.solution: years. The warehouse will save the company $5000 per month in shipping costs, boosting pro(cid:28)t. Maintenance and taxes cost $10,000 per year. At the end of 15 years, what sale price is needed to realize an 8% rate of return? A. $23K B. $230K C. $780K D. $1.7M Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a list of cash (cid:29)ows. In Year 0, there is an initial payment of $500K (negative). In Years 1-15, there is a monthly savings of $5K which translates to a $60K increase to the annual revenue. There is also $10K in annual costs, included taxes. Therefore, the net pro(cid:28)t after tax is (12)($5K)−$10K =$50K per year. InYear15,thereisasalvagevalueofunknownmagnitudewhichisbeingsoughtinthisproblem. Write an expression for the present value. The rate of return is the interest rate that makes the present value equal to zero, in this case, i=8%. Use the i=8% Factor Table to retreive the cash (cid:29)ow factors. Solve for S, the salvage value. PV =−$500K+$50K(P/A,8%,15)+S(P/F,8%,15)=0 −$500K+$50K(8.5595)+S(0.3152)=0 S =$228,506 Answer B 432

problems.statement: A power plant upgrade project takes two years to implement and has an initial cost of $500,000 plus an additional $250,000 at the end of year 1 and year 2. An additional $100,000 retainage will be paid at the end of the one-year defects & liability period following project completion. The life cycle of the upgrade is expected to be 20 years from completion and the salvage value will be $300,000. At an interest rate of 6%, what is the annualized cost of the venture?
problems.solution: has an initial cost of $500,000 plus an additional $250,000 at the end of year 1 and year 2. An additional $100,000 retainage will be paid at the end of the one-year defects & liability period following project completion. The life cycle of the upgrade is expected to be 20 years from completion and the salvage value will be $300,000. At an interest rate of 6%, what is the annualized cost of the venture? A. $83,000 B. $91,000 C. $99,000 D. $107,000 Draw a cash (cid:29)ow diagram or make a summary of cash (cid:29)ows. This solution treats costs as positive. Year 0: $500K Year 1: $250K Year 2: $250K Year 3: $100K Year 20: -$300K The cash (cid:29)ows in years 1 through 3 can be expressed as an annual cost of $100K for 3 years plus an annual cost of an additional $150K for the (cid:28)rst 2 years only. Use the 6% Factor Table in the Economic Analysis section to (cid:28)nd the present value: $500,000+$100,000(P/A,6%,3)+$150,000(P/A,6%,2)−$300,000(P/F,6%,20) $500,000+$100,000(2.673)+$150,000(1.8334)−$300,000(.3118)=$948,770 Find the equivalent annualized cost spread over 20 years at 6%: $948,770(A/P,6%,20)=$948,770(.0872)=$82,733 Alternate Approach: Discount each cash (cid:29)ow back to its present value individually: PV =$500,000 0 (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)n (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)1 PV =$250,000 =$250,000 =$235,849 1 1+i 1.06 (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)n (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)2 PV =$250,000 =$250,000 =$222,499 2 1+i 1.06 (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)n (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)3 PV =$100,000 =$250,000 =$83,962 3 1+i 1.06 433 (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)n (cid:18) 1 (cid:19)20 PV =−$300,000 =−$300,000 =−$93,541 20 1+i 1.06 Take the sum to (cid:28)nd the present value: PV +PV +PV +PV +PV 0 1 2 3 20 $500,000+$235,849+$222,499+$83,962−$93,541=$948,769 Use the same approach as in the original solution to (cid:28)nd the annualized cost: $948,769(A/P,6%,20)=$948,770(.0872)=$82,733 Answer A

problems.statement: The air horsepower produced by a fan is 6.3hp. The fan has a mechanical effciency of 80% and the fan motor has an effciency of 95%. The fan runs for 12 hours per day. What is annual electricity consumption for the fan? 95
problems.solution: mechanical e(cid:30)ciency of 80% and the fan motor has an e(cid:30)ciency of 95%. The fan runs for 12 hours per day. What is annual electricity consumption for the fan? A. 21,000kWh B. 27,000kWh C. 36,000kWh D. 54,000kWh To(cid:28)ndtheelectricalconsumption, startby(cid:28)ndingtheelectricaldemandbydividingtheairhorse- power by both the fan e(cid:30)ciency and the motor e(cid:30)ciency and converting from hp to KW. (cid:16) (cid:17) (6.3hp) 0.7457KW W˙ = AHP = hp =6.18KW η η (0.8)(0.95) f m Find the annual consumption by multiplying the demand by the amount of time the fan runs throughout the year. (cid:18) (cid:19) hr Consumption=(6.18KW) 12 (365days)=27,074kWh day Answer B 434

problems.statement: A fan delivers 10,000cfm of 70◦F air at sea level. How much volume will the same fan deliver when used at 4000ft elevation to distribute 50◦F air?
problems.solution: will the same fan deliver when used at 4000ft elevation to distribute 50◦F air? A. 8,000cfm B. 9,000cfm C. 11,000cfm D. 12,000cfm Qualitatively, itstandstoreasonthatincreasingtheelevationatwhichthefanisusedwillincrease thecapacityofthefanintermsofvolume(cid:29)owratebecauseairislessdensehigherintheatmosphere. However, reducing the temperature of the air makes it more dense, and reduces the capacity of the fan. These factors will compete and must be considered separately. Search for Temperature and Altitude Corrections and use the table to look up the Density Factors for both the temperature and elevation change. The Density Factor for the temperature requires interpolation. Temperature [◦F] Density Factor 0 1.152 50 DF T 70 1 70−50 1−DF = T =0.2857 70−0 1−1.152 1−DF =−0.0434→DF =1.043 T T Look up the Density Factor for the altitude. DF =0.864 A Although it is not explicitly stated in the reference handbook whether the original cfm should be multiplied or divided by the density factors, the previous reasoning provides the expectation that the decreased temperature will tend to reduce the fan capacity and the increased altitude will tend to increase the fan capacity. Therefore, the original cfm should be divided by the density factors. Q 10,000cfm Q = sealevel = =11,097cfm 4000ft DF ·DF (1.043)(0.864) T A Answer C 435

problems.statement: A heat recovery ventilator is used to pre-heat 30◦F, 40% RH outside air with 75◦F, 50% RH exhaust air. The HRV e(cid:27)ectiveness is 60%. What quantity of heat is recovered? lb lb lb lb
problems.solution: air with 75◦F, 50% RH exhaust air. The HRV e(cid:27)ectiveness is 60%. What quantity of heat is recovered? A. 4.3Btu lb B. 6.5Btu lb C. 10.8Btu lb D. 27.1Btu lb Let State 1 refer to the entering outside air condition. Let State 2 refer to the leaving outside air after being heated through the ventilator. Let State 3 refer to the entering return air condition. Ignore the leaving exhaust air as it is not relevant. Recall the distinction between heat recovery and energy recovery. Energy recovery devices transmitlatentenergyinadditiontosensibleheat. Heat-RecoverydevicesdriveexclusivelySensible Energy Recovery and the humidity need not be considered. Therefore, the e(cid:27)ectivenss of a heat recovery ventilator is given by a the ratio of ∆T to ∆T . (Energy recovery e(cid:27)ectiveness actual ideal would depend on changes in enthalpy rather than temperature.) ∆T T −30◦F ε= actual = 2 =0.6 ∆T 75◦F −30◦F ideal T =57◦F 2 The total heat transfer by the heat recovery device is given by the equation below. Sicne there is no mass (cid:29)ow rate or volume rate given and the problem is asking for the quantity of heat rather than the rate of heat transfer, divide both sides by m˙ and solve for q, heat per unit mass. The delta T is the actual increase in temperature experienced by the outside air. Q˙ =m˙c ∆T p Q˙ (cid:18) Btu (cid:19) Btu =q =c ∆T = 0.24 (57◦F −30◦F)=6.48 m˙ p lb·◦F lb Answer B 436

problems.statement: The maximum pressure achieved in the cylinder of a car engine is 800psi. How much force will be exerted on a 3.7in piston? f f f f
problems.solution: 800psi. How much force will be exerted on a 3.7in piston? A. 700lb f B. 2,200lb f C. 4,300lb f D. 8,600lb f Ausefulrepresentationofpressureistheamountofforceappliedoveranarea. Thiscanbeexpressed through the formula below and rearranged to solve for the force, F. F P = A F =PA Determine the area of the piston. π π A= D2 = (3.7in)2 =10.75in2 4 4 Solve for the force. (cid:18) (cid:19) F =PA= 800 lb f (cid:0) 10.75in2(cid:1) =8600lb in2 f Answer D

problems.statement: A spring with 10 coils has squared ends and a shear modulus of 10×106psi. The diameter of the wire is 0.15in and the average coil diameter is 1in. What is the spring constant? 52lbf in 63lbf in 79lbf in 105lbf in 96
problems.solution: 10×106psi. The diameter of the wire is 0.15in and the average coil diameter is 1in. What is the spring constant? A. 52lbf in B. 63lbf in C. 79lbf in D. 105lbf in The Spring Constant for a Helical Compression Spring can be determined using the following formula, where k is the spring constant, d is the diameter of the wire, G is the shear modulus, D is the coil diameter, and Nis the number of active coils. d4G k = 8D3N Using the table Type of Spring Ends, note that for squared ends the total number of coils N is t the number of active coils plus two. Solve for the number of active coils. 437 N =N +2 t N =N −2=10−2=8 t Solve for the spring constant. (cid:16) (cid:17) k = d4G = (0.15in)4 10×106 i l n bf 2 =79 lb f 8D3N 8(1in)3(8) in Answer C

problems.statement: An air handling unit uses 10% outside air at 88◦F and 60% RH and 90% recirculated air returned from the space, which is maintained at 76◦F and 50% RH. What is the dew point temperature of the air entering the coil?
problems.solution: 90% recirculated air returned from the space, which is maintained at 76◦F and 50% RH. What is the dew point temperature of the air entering the coil? A. 56◦F B. 58◦F C. 63◦F D. 65◦F De(cid:28)ne State 1 as the outside air, State 2 as the return air, and State 3 as the mixed air. The question does not concern the supply/discharge air after the coil. Use the Psychrometric Chart to look up the humidity ratio for State 1 and State 2 which are fully de(cid:28)ned. T =88◦F 1 RH =60% 1 lb ω =.0172 H2O 1 lb da T =76◦F 2 RH =50% 2 lb ω =.0096 H2O 2 lb da Perform a mixing calculation to (cid:28)nd the humidity ratio at State 3. 438 (cid:18) (cid:19) (cid:18) (cid:19) lb lb lb ω =(.1) .0172 H2O +(.9) .0096 H2O =.01036 H2O 3 lb lb lb da da da Follow the psychrometric chart horizonally to the left from ω to the saturation curve to read 3 the corresponding dew point temperature. T ≈58◦F DP,3 Answer B

problems.statement: 2000cfm of outside air at 95◦F dry bulb and 78◦F wet bulb is cooled to 68◦F and 60% relative humidity. What quantity of latent heat is removed? hr hr hr hr
problems.solution: to 68◦F and 60% relative humidity. What quantity of latent heat is removed? A. 60,000Btu hr B. 70,000Btu hr C. 80,000Btu hr D. 90,000Btu hr Let state 1 be the entering air conditions and state 2 be the leaving air conditions. Use the Psychrometric Chart to (cid:28)nd the humidity ratio for both states. problems.solution: using a parallel (cid:29)ow plate and frame heat exchanger instead of its chillers. The system consists of two loops, (1) an open condenser water loop which is fed by cooling towers outside, and (2) a closed chilled water loop which is distributed throughout the facility. B Since this is a parallel (cid:29)ow heat exchanger, the temperature di(cid:27)erence between the two (cid:29)uids upon entering, ∆T , is as large as it is ever going to be; i.e. the warm (cid:29)uid is at its warmest and A the cold (cid:29)uid is at its coldest. Therefore: ∆T =60◦F −40◦F =20◦F A The temperature di(cid:27)erence between the two (cid:29)uids upon leaving, ∆T , is as small as it is ever B going to be; i.e. the warm (cid:29)uid has cooled, the cool (cid:29)uid had warmed, and the temperatures ap- proach oneanotherfromeitherside,nevercrossingforaparallel(cid:29)owheatexchanger,butpotentially becoming quite close. ∆T =48◦F −46◦F =2◦F B Solve for the LMTD. problems.statement: A make up pump that cycles on and o(cid:27) intermittently as required has a service factor of 1.25 and provides a water horsepower of 15hp. The pump effciency is 84%. problems.solution: has a service factor of 1.25 and provides a water horsepower of 15hp. The pump e(cid:30)ciency is 84%. The pump is driven by an electric motor with an e(cid:30)ciency of 93%. What is the smallest motor that could be used? A. 10hp B. 15hp C. 20hp D. 25hp The service factor allows a pump to be run harder than its brake horsepower rating provided the pump is not used continuously, therefore the service factor is applicable for the intermittent use case. The water horsepower, whp, and pump e(cid:30)ciency can be used to determine the required bhp of the motor, before accounting for the service factor. whp 15hp bhp= = =17.9hp η 0.84 p Divide by the service factor to specify the required motor bhp for intermittent use. 17.9hp bhp = =14.3hp SF 1.25 Note the motor e(cid:30)ciency is additional information and not required for the solution. Motors are sized based on bhp only, therefore it is not appropriate to consider electrical losses in the motor unless the question relates to required electrical power. A 15hp motor is su(cid:30)cient. Answer B
